# FYP1-MidReport-F24-012-D-SprintCraft - i211786 Muhammad Shamil Umar

## 1. Introduction

1. Introduction
teams.
1.2 Scope
The project aims to create an intelligent software solution that automates the generation
of user stories from Software Requirements Specification (SRS) documents. By accept-
ing an SRS document and a user prompt as inputs, the software will generate contex-
tually accurate user stories. Key functionalities include parsing and analyzing the SRS,
maintaining context throughout the process, ensuring alignment with the specified re-
quirements and self evaluating the user stories. The software will allow customization
through prompts and will incorporate self-evaluation mechanisms to ensure accurate and
relevant user story generation. Additionally, the tool will maintain a history of interac-
tions for consistent output across sessions. The scope of the project includes developing
a user-friendly interface and a backend engine consisting of fine tuned LLMs working in
harmony.
1.3 Modules
1.3.1 User Input Module
This module allows users (e.g., Product Managers, Business Analysts) to upload an SRS
document along with prompts. It provides a user-friendly interface where users can
specify what kind of user stories they want to generate, whether it’s for functional, non-
functional, or specific sections of the provided SRS.
Key Features:
1. SRS document upload
2. Prompt field for user story specification
3. Input validation for ensuring correct file format and prompt structure
1.3.2 Natural Language Processing (NLP) Engine
The NLP engine processes the uploaded SRS document and interprets the user’s provided
prompt. It extracts key elements such as requirements, actors, and system behaviors from
the SRS and translates them into user stories. The engine ensures that technical language
2
1.3 Modules
is transformed into user-friendly language suitable for user stories.
Key Features:
1. Requirement extraction
2. Contextual understanding of the SRS document
3. Prompt-driven interpretation for tailored user stories
1.3.3 User Story Generation Module
This is the core module responsible for transforming the extracted data from the NLP en-
gine into coherent user stories. It adheres to a predefined format (e.g., “As a [role], I want
[goal], so that [benefit]”). The module ensures that both functional and non-functional
requirements are covered, based on the input provided.
Key Features:
1. Auto-generation of user stories
2. Handling functional and non-functional requirements
3. Customization based on the user’s prompt
1.3.4 Story Review and Editing Module
After generating user stories, this module allows users (e.g.,Product Managers, Business
Analysts, Developers etc.) to review and edit the output. It provides an interface where
users can modify the generated stories, add acceptance criteria, and provide feedback on
any necessary changes or introduce a perspective for context.
Key Features:
1. User story editing
2. Acceptance criteria addition
3. Feedback mechanisms for adjusting story generation rules
3
1. Introduction
1.3.5 V ersion Control and Tracking Module
This module tracks changes made to user stories, allowing users to maintain different
versions. It ensures that the history of changes is available for future reference, aiding
collaboration among team members.
Key Features:
1. Version history tracking
2. Story comparison between versions
3. Collaboration support with multiple users
1.3.6 Security and Authentication Module
This module handles user authentication and security, ensuring that only authorized per-
sonnel can access the system. It includes user login, password management, and session
control features.
Key Features:
1. User authentication and login
2. Role-based access control
3. Session management and encryption
4
1.4 User Classes and Characteristics
User class Description
Product Manager A Product Manager is responsible for overseeing product develop-
ment. They will use the system to input an SRS document and
prompt for generating user stories. Product Managers require de-
tailed and accurate user stories that are aligned with business and
technical requirements.
Software Developer Software Developers use the generated user stories to implement fea-
tures and functionality. Developers rely on clear, technical user sto-
ries with well-defined acceptance criteria and may need the ability to
request revisions or more detailed descriptions.
Business Analyst Business Analysts bridge the gap between stakeholders and the de-
velopment team. Analysts refine the SRS before submission and use
the system to ensure user stories align with business goals. They may
work collaboratively to edit or expand stories as necessary.
QA Tester QA Testers use the user stories to create test cases and scenarios. A
QA Testers primary interaction with the system is to ensure stories
have clearly defined acceptance criteria and are testable.
Stakeholders Stakeholders review the output stories at a high level, ensuring they
align with the project’s vision. Their interaction is primarily for feed-
back and approval, as they may not be involved in the detailed cre-
ation or technical aspects of the stories.
Project Supervisor Project Supervisors monitor overall progress and ensure that user sto-
ries align with defined goals. They check stories for consistency with
the project scope and may use the system to generate reports or sum-
maries based on the stories.
1.4 User Classes and Characteristics
The table 1.41.4 above outlines the various user classes anticipated to interact with the sys-
tem that generates user stories from SRS documents. Each class is defined based on its
role in the product development lifecycle, detailing their interactions with the system and
the specific requirements they have from the generated user stories (Product managers,
Software developers ,etc). Each class has distinct needs, ranging from high-level over-
sight to technical implementation. This classification ensures that the system caters to all
user roles effectively, aligning the output with both business and technical objectives.

2.2 Functional Requirements
2.2.1 User Input
1. The module shall provide functionality to allow users to upload SRS (Software Re-
quirements Specification) documents.
Details:
• The module should support document formats such as .doc, .docx, and .pdf.
• Users should be able to browse their local file system and select the SRS doc-
ument to upload.
• The system must notify users upon successful upload or in case of failure with
appropriate error messages.
2. The module shall provide a prompt input field where users can specify the type of
user stories they want to generate.
Details:
• The prompt field should support text input, allowing users to specify require-
ments for functional, non-functional, or specific sections of the SRS.
• The interface should guide the users with examples or suggestions on how to
write effective prompts.
22
2. Project Requirements
• The system must process and store the prompt for subsequent user story gen-
eration.
3. The module shall validate the uploaded SRS document and the prompt input to en-
sure they meet the required format and structure.
Details:
• The system must check the file type of the uploaded document and reject
unsupported formats with an appropriate error message.
• The prompt field must have validation rules to ensure the prompt meets the
minimum and maximum length requirements.
• The system should provide real-time feedback to users if the uploaded docu-
ment or entered prompt does not meet the required criteria.
• The validation process must be efficient to ensure a smooth user experience.
2.2.2 Natural Language Processing (NLP) Engine
1. The NLP engine shall extract key requirements from the uploaded SRS document.
Details:
• The engine should identify and extract functional and non-functional require-
ments from the SRS document.
• It should recognize and tag different components such as goals, constraints,
and assumptions.
• The engine must handle diverse document structures and terminologies to ac-
curately capture the requirements.
2. The NLP engine shall understand the context of the SRS document to accurately
interpret its content.
Details:
• The engine must be capable of handling complex language constructs and
technical jargon to derive meaningful insights.
3. The NLP engine shall interpret the user’s prompt to generate tailored user stories.
Details:
23
2.2 Functional Requirements
• The engine should process the user’s prompt to understand specific require-
ments for user stories (e.g., focus on particular sections, functional vs. non-
functional).
• It must translate technical language and detailed requirements into clear, user-
friendly language suitable for user stories.
• The generated user stories should be coherent, contextually accurate, and
aligned with the user’s prompt specifications.
• The engine should provide feedback to users if the prompt is ambiguous or
insufficient for generating meaningful user stories.
2.2.3 User Story Generation
1. The module shall automatically generate user stories from the data extracted by the
NLP engine.
Details:
• The system should adhere to a predefined format for user stories, such as “As
a [role], I want [goal], so that [benefit]”.
• The module must ensure that each user story is coherent, concise, and follows
the standard user story structure.
• The generation process should be efficient, providing quick results to the user.
2. The module shall be capable of generating user stories for both functional and non-
functional requirements.
Details:
• The system should distinguish between functional and non-functional require-
ments and generate appropriate user stories for each.
• Functional requirements user stories should focus on describing the actions
and features of the system.
• Non-functional requirements user stories should address performance, secu-
rity, usability, and other quality attributes.
• The module must ensure that all relevant requirements extracted by the NLP
engine are covered in the generated user stories.
3. The module shall allow customization of user stories based on the user’s prompt.
Details:
24
2. Project Requirements
• The module should interpret the user’s prompt to tailor the user stories accord-
ing to the specific instructions provided.
• It should allow customization in terms of focus areas, level of detail, and spe-
cific sections or types of requirements.
• The system must ensure that the generated user stories align with the user’s
prompts while maintaining coherence and clarity.
2.2.4 Story Review and Editing
1. The module shall allow users to review and edit the generated user stories.
Details:
• Users should be able to modify the text of the user stories to better fit their
needs or correct any inaccuracies.
• The interface should provide a user-friendly text editor with basic formatting
options (e.g., bold, italic, underline).
• Changes made by users should be saved and reflected in the final user story
output.
• The system must provide version control to track changes and allow users to
revert to previous versions if needed.
2. The module shall provide mechanisms for users to give feedback on the generated
user stories and suggest adjustments to the generation rules.
Details:
• Users should be able to submit feedback on specific user stories, including
suggestions for improvements or noting any issues.
• The system should collect and analyze feedback to identify common patterns
or areas for improvement in the story generation process.
• The interface should allow users to propose new rules or modifications to ex-
isting rules for generating user stories.
• The module should facilitate communication between users and developers to
ensure feedback is addressed and incorporated into future updates.
25
2.2 Functional Requirements
2.2.5 V ersion Control and Tracking
1. The module shall provide functionality to track all changes made to user stories,
capturing each version as a separate entity.
Details:
• The system should create a new version of a user story every time it is edited
and saved.
• Metadata for each version, including the author of the changes, timestamp,
and a brief description of the changes, must be stored.
• A version history view, listing all versions of a user story in chronological
order, should be available.
• Users should have the ability to revert to any previous version of a user story.
2. The module shall provide functionality for users to compare different versions of a
user story to identify changes.
Details:
• A comparison tool should be available to highlight differences between two
selected versions of a user story.
• The tool should display changes in a side-by-side or inline format for easy
comparison.
• Users must be able to select any two versions from the version history for
comparison.
• Additions, deletions, and modifications between versions should be highlighted.
2.2.6 Security and Authentication
1. The module shall provide functionality for user authentication and login, ensuring
that only authorized personnel can access the system.
Details:
• The system must require users to provide valid credentials (username and
password) for login.
• The module should support multi-factor authentication (MFA) to enhance se-
curity.
26
2. Project Requirements
• Users must be notified of successful login or authentication failure with ap-
propriate error messages.
• The system should log all login attempts, including successful and failed at-
tempts, for auditing purposes.
2. The module shall implement role-based access control (RBAC) to manage user per-
missions and ensure that users only have access to the resources they are authorized
to access.
Details:
• The system must allow administrators to define and manage user roles.
• Each role should have specific permissions associated with it, controlling ac-
cess to different parts of the system.
• Users should be assigned roles based on their responsibilities and level of
access required.
• The system must enforce role-based permissions at all times, preventing unau-
thorized access.
2.3 Non-Functional Requirements
The non-functional requirements for this system define the critical quality attributes that
will ensure the system’s reliability, performance, and overall user experience. These re-
quirements are measurable, ensuring the system meets specific performance benchmarks,
scalability expectations, security standards, and user accessibility needs.
2.3.1 Performance
The system must deliver rapid processing to facilitate real-time usage. From processing
the input document and prompt to generating the users stories and then scoring generated
user stories, the entire process must be fast and precise. An estimated time of 10-15 sec-
onds is desirable. The system allow simultaneous users activities without any observable
delay or significant performance deterioration. This performance metric ensures that the
system remains efficient, even during periods of high demand.
2.3.2 Scalability
As the system is expected to handle growing user bases over time, it must be scalable. The
system should support multiple concurrent users, adapt to increased workload without
27
2.3 Non-Functional Requirements
performance bottlenecks. This scalability should be achieved through horizontal scaling
mechanisms, such as adding more servers or cloud-based resources when necessary.The
system architecture must be structured to accommodate an increased volume of traffic
and/or document size, maintaining optimal performance.
2.3.3 Security
Data security is paramount for this system, given that it handles sensitive project docu-
mentation and proprietary information. All stored data, including SRS documents and
generated user stories, must be encrypted using AES-256 encryption, ensuring maximum
data protection. In addition, the system must implement secure user authentication meth-
ods, following OAuth 2.0 standards to protect user accounts and access controls. Role-
based access control (RBAC) will further ensure that different types of users (e.g., admins,
developers, and reviewers) have appropriate levels of access, preventing unauthorized data
manipulation.
2.3.4 Usability
For the system to be widely used/accepted, it must prioritize user friendliness. The user
interface will be designed to be intuitive and user-friendly, ensuring users can easily up-
load SRS documents and prompts, even without technical knowledge. Compliance with
WCAG 2.1 accessibility standards will be mandatory. Error handling within the system
must be comprehensive, providing users with clear, actionable feedback to correct input
mistakes without external support, thereby ensuring a seamless user experience.
2.3.5 Relibility
The system is expected to function continuously with minimal interruptions. To this end,
it will maintain a service uptime of 90%, ensuring availability for users around the clock.
In the event of a system failure, automatic failover mechanisms must be in place to restore
services within 1-10 minutes of downtime. Additionally, daily backups of user data to
safeguard against data loss or corruption will be implemented, ensuring the ability to
restore the system to a working state without significant data loss.
2.3.6 Maintainability
The system’s architecture must be designed for easy maintenance and future updates. The
codebase should adhere to SOLID design principles, promoting modularity and ease of
28
2. Project Requirements
change. Allowing developers to make adjustments or implement upgrades without sig-
nificant rewrites. Scheduled updates and patches must be deploy-able with minimal dis-
ruption, keeping system downtime to less than 1 hour. Moreover, comprehensive system
logs will track system performance and errors, ensuring that maintenance tasks, such as
troubleshooting and diagnostics, can be completed efficiently.

3.1.1 Box and Line Diagram

3. System Overview
Box and line diagram in Figure 3.13.1 represent how different modules interact with each
other to achieve the system’s overall functionality. Security and Authentication module is
responsible for authentication of each user. Once verified, users can interact with appli-
cation via user input module where they submit SRS document & prompts. These inputs
are stored in database for further processing.
The SRS document analysis step involves extracting necessary details from document
such as functional and non-functional requirements with the help of NLP Engine module.
This process is crucial, as extracted requirements act as primary input for generation of
user stories along with textual prompt given by user.
Once user stories are generated, they go through Story Review and Editing module where
user can view and make modifications according to his/her needs.This module if also con-
nected with version history which allows users to track changes in user stories. All edited
user stories are stored in Database.
To manage different versions of user stories, the system uses Version Control and Track-
ing Module. This module makes sure that each iteration of user stories are recorded for
maintenance of history of changes in chronological order. The architecture ensures that
user stories are not only generated efficiently but also stored securely with robust tracking
system.
32
3.1 Architectural Design
3.1.2 Model, View and Controller (MVC) Diagram
Figure 3.2: MVC diagram for complete architecture
The MVC architecture divides the SprintCraft application into three layers: Model, View,
and Controller. The Model manages data, such as users, SRS, and user stories, along
with version control. The View handles user interaction interfaces, including story gen-
eration, editing, and version history. The Controller acts as a bridge, processing inputs
and updating the Model and Views accordingly. Summary for SprintCraft: The MVC
architecture for SprintCraft organizes the app into three components. The Model man-
ages data and logic, handling user stories, SRS data, version control, and authentication.
The View presents user interfaces for input, story generation, editing, and version his-
tory. The Controller processes user actions, updating both the Model and View to ensure
smooth user story generation and management. This architecture simplifies maintenance
and scalability.

will try to extract Functional and Non-functional requirements.

NLP Engine, as shown in Figure 3.53.5 and Figure 3.63.6 follow a structured workflow that

formation is insufficient in prompt, user is once again notify until both conditions are met.
This unified description shows how the NLP engine facilitates requirement extraction and
user story generation while providing feedback loops for handling errors or ambiguities
at different stages.

This module follows a structured workflow that allow users to review and edit user stories.
This process starts when user open a generated user story for evaluation. The user reviews
the content and determines whether changes are required or not. If user decides to make
changes, user is redirected to editor where they can modify. Once changes are made, the
user will save edits which will be reflected to version control system where details of each
edit will be recorded.
After review, user is asked to submit feedback on the user story. Here they have choice to
either submit rule modification proposal or just submit regular feedback. This feedback
is stored in system for analysis. This step ensures that user input is not only recorded but
also utilized to improve generation of user stories and enhance the rules that guide story
creation.

version of the user story. Along with the new version, the system also stores other im-
portant information such as the author who performed changes, the timestamp, and a
description of the modifications made. This helps in organizing and maintaining the ver-
sion history for future reference.
Once version is created, system automatically updates history. User has option to view
history and if he wishes to view it, system will display it in chronological timeline allow-

restore. The system then reverts the user story to the selected version, effectively undoing
the more recent changes.

3.2.6 Module 6: Security and Authentication Module
Figure 3.13: Module 6: Activity Diagram
44
3.2 Design Models
Figure 3.14: Module 6: System Sequence Diagram
Figure 3.133.13 and Figure 3.143.14 represent flow of this module. It handles user login procedure
and role-based access control (RBAC). When user opens login page of application, he/she
is asked to enter their credentials. Once submitted, the system check if credential are valid
by cross checking it with database. If invalid, user is asked to enter id and password again
and makes sure that user is unable to access any sensitive information.
If credentials are valid, system proceeds to perform Multi Factor Authentication(MFA).
Outcome of this step will decide if user should be allowed to access information or not.
Authentication code is sent via email to user and system will be waiting for user to enter
authentication code. If code entered is invalid, then he will not be allowed to access data
and if code is valid then user will be allowed to access application.
Upon successful login, system check user roles and grants access to resources based on
their role through RBAC. This will make sure that any user can not access data that is not
45
3. System Overview
meant for him. The system then logs all login attempts for auditing purposes, creating
a complete record of both successful and unsuccessful login attempts which will allow
system to check for pattern that might indicate if system is being hacked or not.
The overall design aims to enhance security by integrating multiple layers of validation,
auditing, and role-based access, ensuring proper control over resources that are being
accessed by user.

2.2.1 User Input
1. The module shall provide functionality to allow users to upload SRS (Software Re-
quirements Specification) documents.
Details:
• The module should support document formats such as .doc, .docx, and .pdf.
• Users should be able to browse their local file system and select the SRS doc-
ument to upload.
• The system must notify users upon successful upload or in case of failure with
appropriate error messages.
2. The module shall provide a prompt input field where users can specify the type of
user stories they want to generate.

ments for functional, non-functional, or specific sections of the SRS.

write effective prompts.

2. Project Requirements
• The system must process and store the prompt for subsequent user story gen-
eration.

sure they meet the required format and structure.

unsupported formats with an appropriate error message.

minimum and maximum length requirements.
• The system should provide real-time feedback to users if the uploaded docu-
ment or entered prompt does not meet the required criteria.
• The validation process must be efficient to ensure a smooth user experience.

technical jargon to derive meaningful insights.

2.3 Non-Functional Requirements
The non-functional requirements for this system define the critical quality attributes that
will ensure the system’s reliability, performance, and overall user experience. These re-
quirements are measurable, ensuring the system meets specific performance benchmarks,
scalability expectations, security standards, and user accessibility needs.
2.3.1 Performance
The system must deliver rapid processing to facilitate real-time usage. From processing
the input document and prompt to generating the users stories and then scoring generated
user stories, the entire process must be fast and precise. An estimated time of 10-15 sec-
onds is desirable. The system allow simultaneous users activities without any observable
delay or significant performance deterioration. This performance metric ensures that the
system remains efficient, even during periods of high demand.
2.3.2 Scalability
As the system is expected to handle growing user bases over time, it must be scalable. The
system should support multiple concurrent users, adapt to increased workload without
27
2.3 Non-Functional Requirements
performance bottlenecks. This scalability should be achieved through horizontal scaling
mechanisms, such as adding more servers or cloud-based resources when necessary.The
system architecture must be structured to accommodate an increased volume of traffic
and/or document size, maintaining optimal performance.
2.3.3 Security
Data security is paramount for this system, given that it handles sensitive project docu-
mentation and proprietary information. All stored data, including SRS documents and
generated user stories, must be encrypted using AES-256 encryption, ensuring maximum
data protection. In addition, the system must implement secure user authentication meth-
ods, following OAuth 2.0 standards to protect user accounts and access controls. Role-
based access control (RBAC) will further ensure that different types of users (e.g., admins,
developers, and reviewers) have appropriate levels of access, preventing unauthorized data
manipulation.
2.3.4 Usability
For the system to be widely used/accepted, it must prioritize user friendliness. The user
interface will be designed to be intuitive and user-friendly, ensuring users can easily up-
load SRS documents and prompts, even without technical knowledge. Compliance with
WCAG 2.1 accessibility standards will be mandatory. Error handling within the system
must be comprehensive, providing users with clear, actionable feedback to correct input
mistakes without external support, thereby ensuring a seamless user experience.
2.3.5 Relibility
The system is expected to function continuously with minimal interruptions. To this end,
it will maintain a service uptime of 90%, ensuring availability for users around the clock.
In the event of a system failure, automatic failover mechanisms must be in place to restore
services within 1-10 minutes of downtime. Additionally, daily backups of user data to
safeguard against data loss or corruption will be implemented, ensuring the ability to
restore the system to a working state without significant data loss.
2.3.6 Maintainability
The system’s architecture must be designed for easy maintenance and future updates. The
codebase should adhere to SOLID design principles, promoting modularity and ease of
28
2. Project Requirements
change. Allowing developers to make adjustments or implement upgrades without sig-
nificant rewrites. Scheduled updates and patches must be deploy-able with minimal dis-
ruption, keeping system downtime to less than 1 hour. Moreover, comprehensive system
logs will track system performance and errors, ensuring that maintenance tasks, such as
troubleshooting and diagnostics, can be completed efficiently.

3.1 Architectural Design
3.1.2 Model, View and Controller (MVC) Diagram
Figure 3.2: MVC diagram for complete architecture
The MVC architecture divides the SprintCraft application into three layers: Model, View,
and Controller. The Model manages data, such as users, SRS, and user stories, along
with version control. The View handles user interaction interfaces, including story gen-
eration, editing, and version history. The Controller acts as a bridge, processing inputs
and updating the Model and Views accordingly. Summary for SprintCraft: The MVC
architecture for SprintCraft organizes the app into three components. The Model man-
ages data and logic, handling user stories, SRS data, version control, and authentication.
The View presents user interfaces for input, story generation, editing, and version his-
tory. The Controller processes user actions, updating both the Model and View to ensure
smooth user story generation and management. This architecture simplifies maintenance
and scalability.

Chapter 1 
1. Introduction 
The purpose of this project document is to outline a detailed plan for the development 
of an interactive virtual reality (VR) application that will transform video drone -
footages of residential housing society into a 3D model for a virtual tour. The 
document will outline the project's problem we are going to address, what motivat ed 
us, scope of our project, deliverables, and all the technical processes involved in 
bringing an innovative solution to overcome the problem which we are going to 
implement in our final year project. The detailed overview of the project would mainly 
focus on creating a virtual environment of residential housing societies. This is a VR 
based application, which will provide a platform for stakeholders such as real estate 
builders associated with the society or society management to give a virtual tour of the 
society environment to the customers who are interested in investing in the society. A 
web application will be provided to the stakeholders, where they can upload video 
drone footage which will be used to generate a 3D model of the environment. The 3D 
model will be accessible through a VR based application using VR headset to virtually 
tour the environment. This project will help bridge the gap in the industry by providing 
an innovative solution to visualize and explore residential societies using modern-day 
technologies. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Page 6 of 36 
 
Chapter 2 
2. Project Vision 
2.1 Problem Statement 
The real estate market in Pakistan is growing at an unprecedented pace, with investors 
seeking safer, more Secure investments. Yet despite its rapid expansion, the industry 
still relies heavily on Traditional methods of Property visualization —photos, 2D ma ps, 
and occasional video tours. In traditional methods of exploring and assessing a new 
Residential society, individuals must physically visit each street and corner to get a 
comprehensive understanding of the area. This process is not only Time-consuming and 
physically demanding, but also impractical for those with Mobility limitations or busy 
schedules. The existing approaches to remotely visualize residential properties and 
societies, for instance, 'Google Street View and static 2D maps,' are inadequate to 
provide the required flexibility and interactivity for detailed exploration. Such models do 
not get updated in real -time and are very expensive in their development and 
maintenance; hence, not sufficient for the Customer, real estate developers, and 
investors who base their decisions on engaging, instructive visualizations. This indeed 
calls for a more efficient, interactive way of turning Drone footage into highly detailed, 
immersive 3D models. Increasingly, TerraVerse 3D meets this challenge with Virtu al 
Reality and AI-driven technology to power the generation of 3D models from drone video 
footage. The system has been speeding up the whole process more economically and 
much more attractively. 
 
 
 
 
 
Page 7 of 36 
 
2.2 Business Opportunity 
Educational and Training Purposes: 
• Architectural and Engineering Education: Various universities and training 
institutes can use TerraVerse 3D to depict several real examples related to town 
planning and architectural designs. 
• Professional Training: VR tours can be used in training by real estate 
professionals to understand the layout of the property and various sales 
techniques. 
Real Estate Marketing and Sales: 
• Virtual Property Tours: Through virtual property tours, real estate agents and 
developers allow potential buyers to take a personal tour of their properties 
without having to go there physically. This ensures better and quicker decision -
making and decreases the time a property spends on the market. 
• Improved Customer Interaction: Meet customers in a more interactive manner, 
attracting more buyers to view properties and result in higher sales 
International Real Estate Investment: 
• Cross-Border Viewings: TerraVerse 3D will be able to allow buyers and investors 
from any part of the world to view properties located in other regions virtually 
without having to physically visit the area. This will increase the market reach 
manifold for developers and real estate agents. 
• Enhanced Decision-Making: By providing detailed and immersive virtual tours of 
properties, international investors can make more informed decisions about 
purchasing properties abroad. This can reduce the risk associated with investing 
in unfamiliar markets.

● Generate 3D models from drone footage using Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) 
● Develop a VR environment compatible with Oculus Rift 
● Implement an AI -powered Q&A system to provide information about the 3D 
society

2.4 Project Scope 
1. Data Collection and Preprocessing 
● Stabilizing and Preprocessing the Video Footage: 
o Video stabilization software will be used to remove unwanted movements 
and jitters from footage. 
o Footage will be preprocessed by correcting lens distortions, adjusting 
lighting, and image quality will be enhanced for better 3D reconstruction. 
2. 3D Model Reconstruction 
● Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF): 
o NeRF: We will use neural networks to illustrate the model in 3D 
environment, allowing for highly detailed and realistic 3D reconstructions. 
● Optimizing the 3D Model for VR Performance: 
o Simplifying the 3D model by reducing the polygon count while important 
details are being preserved. 
o Optimizing lighting and applying texture maps so that to enhance realism 
without compromising performance. 
o Ensuring that the model meets VR performance standards and providing a 
smooth and immersive experience. 
3. VR Application Development

Page 12 of 36 
 
 2.4 Constraints 
Technical Constraints: 
● Hardware Limitations : The VR application requires users to have access to 
compatible VR headsets (e.g., Oculus Rift) and powerful enough hardware to run 
the application smoothly. 
User Constraints: 
● Accessibility: Ensuring the VR application is easy-to-use and accessible to 
everyone with varying levels of technical expertise and physical abilities. 
Scalability Constraints: 
● Multiple Societies: Initially, the project will focus on a single residential society, 
and expanding to multiple societies will require significant additional resources 
and planning. We will be covering 500 kanal of landmark. 
Real-time 3D Reconstruction: 
• Definition: The whole project is based only on pre-recorded footage from drones 
to create the 3D model of the residential society. Real-time video processing and 
reconstruction is not implemented. 
• Explanation: This limitation allows the project to focus on the accuracy and 
quality of the 3D models created from captured footage, instead of developing a 
complex system for real-time processing. By relying on pre-recorded footage, the 
project will create a more controlled environment for model generation, resulting 
in better overall quality. However, it constrains the application's ability to react to 
dynamic changes within the environment. 
 
 
Page 13 of 36 
 
Multi-Society Implementation: 
We will be considering only one residential society for the initial phase of the project and 
will expand to multiple societies in the pipeline for upcoming phases. 
Extensive AI Training: 
We will train NLP to provide information related to the specific residential society only, 
using the data that is shared by that society. 
Model Updates: 
We will create only one version of the 3D model of the residential society and no further updates 
or revisions over time. 
 2.6 Targeted Users 
Property Managers and Real Estate Agents: 
• Usage: It will enable users to virtually expose already existing properties to 
potential renters or buyers. This will make multiple listings easier to handle. 
• Benefits: Smoothens the process to view property without much need for 
physical visits and expands the ability to reach a larger audience. 
Potential Homebuyers and Investors: 
• Usage: Browse properties and evaluate them from a distance with the best 
possible realistic view of your future potential investment without having to travel 
to it. 
• Benefits: Offers convenience, efficiency, and better decision -making since 
buyers can experience properties in a virtual environment.

provide relevant answers, based on the data the model will receive.

navigability and low latency.

Availability:

application and VR features are always up and accessible for users and

Reliability: 
• The system shall ensure that the latency in authenticating a user and establishing 
a network session is below 50 milliseconds to present a consistent response.

how to move in the VR space and understand the application features with 
minimal training.

within a maximum time of 30 minutes for a typical residential society (up to 500 
kanal). 
• The VR application shall maintain a frame rate of at least 60 frames per second 
(FPS) to ensure smooth navigation and an immersive experience.

and a ticketing system for user inquiries.

# FYP1-MidReport-F24-033-D-Emit - i211563 Hadia Shahid

## 1. Introduction

1. Introduction
we will integrate it onto our detection engine, Emit. This will allow us to detect insider
threats both statically and using AI model.
Figure 1.1: Architecture of Emit
2
1.3 Modules
1.3 Modules
1.3.1 Module 1: Identification of Threats and Agent Creation
The first part of our project is all about getting a clear picture of insider threats by iden-
tifying and labeling different behaviors that could signal a risk. We’ll start by building a
detailed list of these behaviors to help us understand what to look for. After this, we’ll
move on to setting up and configuring monitoring agents that will track and collect the
necessary data. Once everything is set up, these agents will be deployed across our sys-
tems, allowing us to detect and report potential threats in real-time.
1. Identifying Threats And Behaviors: This part of the module includes creating an
understanding of how insider threats work and the pattern that follows. We will be
documenting the threats and behaviors that will allow us to configure our agents to
search for the right logs in the end systems.
• Identification of threats: This includes identifying insider threats and docu-
menting them for future use.
• Understanding behaviors that lead to attacks: This includes understanding
the behaviors that eventually lead to insider attacks.
• Identifying what logs are needed: After documentation and understanding
of insider attacks, we will be able to identify what type of logs we need to
extract from our systems.
2. Agent Creation: This part of the module focuses on creating agents according
to the understanding we have developed from the previous work. The following
actions will be performed:
• Agent Creation: This step includes writing the script as a framework for log
generation as well as a small application that can be downloaded.
• Agent Configuration: After creation of agents, we will configure them ac-
cording to our needs and what data they will extract for us from the systems
in our network.
• Agent Deployment: After Configurations, these agents will be deployed in
multiple end systems and then tested.
3
1. Introduction
Figure 1.2: Agent creation
1.3.2 Module 2: Dataset Handling and Static Rule Detection
The second module will focus on creating a dataset from the logs generated by the de-
ployed agents. This dataset will be labeled to categorize different types of insider threat
behaviors. Simultaneously, the logs will also undergo static rule detection, where prede-
fined rules will be applied to detect threats in real-time.
1. Dataset Creation: The second module will focus on creating a dataset from the
logs generated by the deployed agents. This dataset will be labeled to categorize
different types of insider threat behaviors. Simultaneously, the logs will also un-
dergo static rule detection, where predefined rules will be applied to detect threats
in real-time.
• Storing Logs: Logs received from agents will be stored in a database.
• Pre-processing of Logs: This step involves cleaning the logs to remove irrel-
evant and dirty data, also giving logs a structure and format.
• Identifying Fields: We will identify what fields we need in our dataset.
• Dataset Creation: After these steps, the dataset finalization will be on the
way.
2. Dataset Labelling: This part focuses on assigning labels to the dataset created
from the logs. It will include the following steps:
• Labeling Threat Categories: Labelling threats intensity according to a scale.
• Tagging Event Types: Categorizing log events
• Validation of Labels: Revision to check if the labelling was correct.
3. Static Rule Detection: This part involves applying static rule detection on the logs
to identify predefined threats. It will include the following steps:
• Rule Set Creation: This step will involve identifying the rules needed, and
then creating a rule set.
4
1.3 Modules
• Implementing Rules: These rules will be enforced onto the logs using our
engine.
• Monitoring & Alerting: Threat detection will be underway after the above
two features are implemented.
• Rule Refinement: Reviews of rule set to keep us updated on newer threats.
Figure 1.3: Data labelling and static rule detection
5
1. Introduction
1.3.3 Module 3: Automation
The third module focuses on automating the detection process by incorporating machine
learning models. This begins with training the model using the dataset created from the
logs, where the labeled data will allow the model to learn patterns associated with insider
threats. Once the model is trained and evaluated for accuracy, it will be fine-tuned to
minimize false positives and enhance its detection capabilities. Afterward, the automated
detection will be integrated into the SIEM system’s detection engine, enabling real-time
identification of insider threats.
1. Model Training: This part involves using the dataset created and labelled earlier to
train our AI model. As for now, we will not be looking at deep learning models as
there is no need for them. The classification model isn’t defined as of yet, because
we will analyze that as we move forward in our progress.
2. Integrating Automation: This part involves using the trained AI model to detect
real time threats by integrating it onto our detection engine. After this, our engine
will be capable of both, static and AI assisted detection and monitoring.
1.3.4 Module 4: Web Application
The fourth module involves the development of a web application that will serve as the
interface for the SIEM system. The features and the application architecture are follow-
ing:
• Features:
1. User Dashboards: Customizable dashboards displaying real-time security
alerts, system performance metrics, and key insights.
2. Alert Visualization: Interactive visualizations of alerts and threat patterns,
including charts, graphs, and heatmaps.
3. Log Management: Access to detailed logs with search, filter, and export
functionalities for in-depth analysis.
4. Incident Management: Tools for tracking and managing security incidents,
including case management and workflow automation.
5. Integration with Detection Systems: Seamless integration with both auto-
mated detection and static rule-based systems for comprehensive threat mon-
itoring.
6. User Access Control: Role-based access control to ensure that users have
appropriate permissions based on their roles and responsibilities.
6
1.3 Modules
7. Real-Time Notifications: Alerts and notifications sent to users for immediate
response to detected threats or system anomalies.
• Appllication Architecture: The web application architecture will be designed to
ensure a seamless and efficient interaction between the user interface, backend ser-
vices, and data storage. The architecture includes the following components:
1. User Dashboards: Customizable dashboards displaying real-time security
alerts, system performance metrics, and key insights.
2. Frontend (UI): Developed using React, the frontend will provide a dynamic
and responsive user interface. React’s component-based architecture will en-
able efficient rendering and interaction with the application, including dash-
boards, visualizations, and log management tools.
3. Backend: Node.js will be used for the backend services, handling server-
side logic, data processing, and communication between the frontend and the
database. Node.js’s non-blocking architecture will ensure scalability and per-
formance.
4. Database: MongoDB will serve as the database, storing logs, user data, and
configuration settings. Its flexible schema design will accommodate various
types of data and support efficient querying and indexing.
5. APIs: RESTful APIs will be implemented to facilitate communication be-
tween the frontend and backend. These APIs will handle requests for data
retrieval, updates, and interaction with the detection systems.
6. Integration: The backend will integrate with the automated detection engine
and static rule detection systems to provide real-time data and alerts. It will
also manage user authentication and authorization, ensuring secure access to
the application’s features.
7

2. Project Requirements
2.2 Functional Requirements
2.2.1 Module 1: Agent Creation
Following are the requirements for Module 1:
FR1: The system shall provide a designed environment for the creation of the monitoring
agents that collect and generate logs from the computer endpoints.
FR2: The system shall be designed to enable scripting agent development using lan-
guages such as Python and PowerShell to increase usability.
FR3: The system shall provide information for the developers on creating agents which
will include templates and examples.
FR4: The system shall provide a configuration file or interface for setting parameters
such as: types of logs collection, data collection frequency, filters of certain event
or threshold.
FR5: The system shall permit the definition of agents by allowing the configuration of
agents to determine if the agent will be deployed in polling mode or real-time.
FR6: The system shall have a methodology in place to address testing of each agent
deployed in the production environment.
FR7: The framework shall contain the ability to run and create various scenarios to test
the agents and confirm they work under those situations.
FR8: The system shall provide a facility to the researcher to deploy the agent on more
than one end system, methods including: scripts that can be executed from com-
mand line for deployment purposes.
FR9: There are automated deployment options that can be done with the use of configu-
ration management tools like Ansible, Puppet, etc.
2.2.2 Module 2: Logs Management
Following are the requirements for Module 2:
FR1: The system shall have tools for retaining, in the central repository, logs that are
generated by deployed agents.
FR2: The system will support the Classification Shelves and Dunnage Warehousing Sys-
tems for logs of varying capacity.
22
2.2 Functional Requirements
FR3: Cleans logs by stamping out all non-essential or damaged data elements.
FR4: Standardizes the log entries to maintain global order.
FR5: The system should have a module for log records of preprocessing heads to support
audits.
FR6: The system shall have tools to detect threats using a rule set.
2.2.3 Module 3: Model Training
Following are the requirements for Module 3:
FR1: The system will employ the labeled dataset developed in Module 2 in the training
of the machine learning model.
FR2: The system will implement suitable methods to efficiently prepare and load the
dataset in a training application.
FR3: The system will incorporate the performance evaluation of the classifier algorithms,
and a decision will be made on the selected algorithms, for example, decision trees,
random forests, and support vector machines.
FR4: The system will provide a facility for setting up the model parameters as well as the
model hyperparameters pertinent to the algorithm deployed.
FR5: The system will introduce a training infrastructure capable of enabling function-
alities related to the carrying out of the model training process with the selected
algorithm and dataset in place.
FR6: The system will provide for dataset partitioning into training, validation, and test
sets for purposes of testing the prediction accuracy of the model developed.
FR7: The system will perform and include a set of evaluation metrics for the model in
terms of performance, for instance, accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC-
ROC, among others.
FR8: The system will also offer means of illustrating the results of the evaluation carried
out on the model such that it will be easier for the analysts to comprehend the
usefulness of the model.
FR9: The system shall allow adjustments to certain dimensions of the model, revisiting
and making corrections based on evaluation results.
FR10: The system shall allow integration of the AI model based detection engine into the
main application.
23
2. Project Requirements
2.2.4 Module 4: User Dashboards
Following are the requirements for Module 4:
FR1: The system shall provide users with customizable dashboards to display real-time
security alerts and system performance metrics.
FR2: The system shall allow users to rearrange and configure dashboard widgets accord-
ing to their preferences.
FR3: The dashboard shall include widgets for displaying key insights, such as the number

2.3 Non-Functional Requirements
2.3.1 Reliability
Considering the consequences of missed detections or false positives, our solution will
have to achieve a very high level of reliability as far as the detection of insider threats is
concerned. Reliability is defined by the Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF), particu-
larly in the areas of collecting and processing the logs.
25
2. Project Requirements
• Reliability Goal: The system is designed with the assumption that its MTBF will
not be less than 6 months in such a manner that active surveillance of the logs will
be done with minimum interruptions.
• Failure Definition: The term failure for emit means the perceived failure of the
system to gather, handle, or interpret the logs, or positive/negative risk assessments
that could seriously impair the performance of the system.
• Error Correction Strategy: If the log processing is unsuccessful, the host-based
agents will be reconfigured to affect a self-recovery by the system. In the event of
a clinically dead error persistent log collection will be manually triggered.
2.3.2 Usability
For security analysts and system administrators, the system should be simple to under-
stand, use, and engage with.
• USE-1: With no more than three interactions within the dashboard, the system
should enable security analysts to examine and evaluate the alerts generated.
• USE-2: With a maximum response time of two seconds for each interaction, the
web application should include real-time visualizations that let users swiftly evalu-
ate the state of insider threat detection and recognize patterns in insider attacks that
occur within their organization.
• USE-3: Security analysts should be able to view the list of users being monitored
within one interaction.
2.3.3 Performance
Emit needs to continue operating at a high level, particularly when managing logs and
immediately identifying insider threats with a minimum rate of false positives.
• PER-1: Incoming logs should be processed and analyzed by the system in real
time, with a 5 second processing limit for log entries from any host system.
• PER-2: Threat warnings must be displayed by the web application two seconds
after they are detected.
• PER-3: Without compromising speed, Emit should enable the smooth log consoli-
dation of all host-based agents.
• PER-4: Emit should minimize the rate of false positives, through behavioral anal-
ysis.
26
2.4 Domain Model
2.3.4 Security
Safety has to be aligned with system protection as well as data processed by our solution,
which emphasizes the detection and mitigation of insider threats.
• SEC-1: The system shall resist, at a minimum, the best efforts of an attacker for
a period of at least 8 hours in making an unauthorized access from the external
environment.
• SEC-2: All host-based agents must collect log and have encryption-in-transit and
at rest for confidentiality and integrity.
• SEC-3: The system shall implement RBAC: the only access control mechanism;
prohibit operations other than viewing, modification, or deletion of sensitive log
data from being accessed by authorized persons.
• SEC-4: Any attempt to gain unauthorized access or modify the system should raise
an alarm to the security team within less than 5 seconds after detection.
2.4 Domain Model

Chapter 3
System Overview
3.1 Architectural Design
The Emit Agent first collects system and network logs from employees and sends them to
the selected SIEM for aggregation. Once aggregated, these logs are forwarded to Emit,
where the logs are then forked and sent in parallel to the Static Rule Checker and Be-
havioral Analysis modules. The Static Rule Checker analyzes logs against predefined
rules, while the Behavioral Analysis module uses an AI-based Anomaly Model to de-
tect suspicious patterns or behaviors checking multiple logs from users. If either module
detects a threat, an event is generated and sent to the Dashboard for review. Detected
events are also stored in the database for future analysis, and the SOC Team can create
new rules based on flagged behaviors, enhancing Emit’s ability to identify insider threats
over time. Moreover, HR can Utilize the HR Flagging System to identify any employees
that may be deemed High Risk. This Risk is sent to the Checkers who will then increase
the severity of each action / event.

3.3 Data Design
3.3 Data Design
3.3.1 Transform and Structure Data
Information Flow:
The Emit component deals with processes, controls, and supervision or monitoring of
any threats posed by users and their behaviors. Data logs are collected by agents and
their output is integrated by a SIE (Security Information and Event Management) system
while the Behavioral Model performs data processing in an order to identify any potential
threats. This threat information is uploaded and retrieved in the SOC (Security Operation
Center) dashboard.
Data Structure:
Window, User, Agent, Log, Thread and Behavior are examples of entities which are
maintained in separate containers enabling fast processing of data and tracking of their
inter-relationships.
Data Transformation:
Agent Logs are sent to a SIEM where they are merged and scrutinized. Threats and events
are identified and put in reserve for analysis and actions by the SOC. Data Resourcing,
Processing and Arrangement Log Collection: Agents are supposed to capture activity logs
which are sent to a Log Data Store and further consolidated by a SIEM (Wazuh).
Threat Detection:
AI enhances the capacity of the Behavioral Model to scrutinize the conjoined logs and
narrow down on probable threats identified by abnormal behavior. Static rule content
eliminates known threats and issues within the SIEM and offers immediate notifications.
Alerting and Monitoring:
Any threats which have been identified are put aside and placed in the alert database.
Alerts and users put on alert by the HR department are integrated and shown to Admin /
SOC for Security clearance.
37
3. System Overview
3.3.2 Information Flow and Transformation
Integration and Processing:
The Emit component handles the processes, controls, and supervision of threats posed by
user behaviors. Data logs, collected by agents, are processed and integrated by a SIEM
system, while the Behavioral Model performs data analysis to identify potential threats.
This information is subsequently uploaded to and retrievable from the SOC dashboard.
Data Structure and Transformation:
• Data Structure: Entities such as Window, User, Agent, Log, Thread, and Behavior
are maintained in separate containers to facilitate fast data processing and tracking
of interrelationships.

of operations that can be conducted. Furthermore, the existing solutions lack flexibility

of evasion. The primary operation encompasses the choice of the distinction methods in-
cluding variable encryption, full code encryption, garbage code and import obfuscation.

lation, metamorphism, and sandboxing so that malware escapes any A V , EDR, and XDR.

## 1. Introduction

1. Introduction
due to a lack of developer knowledge and students not adjusting to industry standards of
coding. This leads to increased costs of maintaining the code and leaves it vulnerable to
attackers that result in data theft or completely choking the system. Without effective tools
that ensure quality, developers cannot maintain quality; even if there are good developers,
there is always room for error. Static code analysis tools exist, but they lack understanding
of the code’s intent and context. Current static analysis tools provide some level of assis-
tance, but they are often limited in scope, lack context awareness, and frequently produce
a high number of false positives or overlook critical issues altogether. Continuous updates
with the latest data need to be integrated into a tool that can understand the context.
1.2 Scope
Reagent aims to provide a coding interface in the PyCharm IDE for Python code that
performs live code standard detection along with vulnerability and threat detection for
novice programmers. It aims to help programmers learn the industry standard of coding
and to rid their code of any vulnerabilities. Reagent will include a simple and effective AI
model that will suggest code-style fixes and vulnerabilities. For example, if the AI detects
SQL-based vulnerability will highlight the line in the code and then suggest a possible
fix. Reagent will help detect coding structure mistakes according to the PEP8 standards.
Reagent will be powered by Llama 3 open-source AI model catered to specific coding
styles and vulnerabilities. Being a plugin, it will provide seamless integration into Py-
Charm is a popular IDE for Python programmers. Along with code style and threat detec-
tion, Reagent will also provide a code narration feature that will create a short summary
of the code. The narration will help other programmers understand the motive behind the
code and ease the process of re-usability. Writing clean code with minimal vulnerabilities
is one thing, but if code efficiency is compromised, the code becomes unusable. There-
fore, Reagent will also help reduce the time complexity of the code so that it remains
efficient and fast.
Reagent will provide live suggestions for code fixes by using popups and in-line sug-
gestions, much like the GitHub co-pilot UI. Additionally, the plugin will provide a user-
friendly interface for a seamless coding experience. By narrowing the focus to code
standards and vulnerabilities, Reagent aims to address the specific challenges beginners
face while coding, making it an accessible and specialized tool for users across different
industries.
2

This module aims to develop a model using PyTorch based on LLaMA 3. The model will
be designed to test Python code against PEP8 standards and check for common security
threats. This approach is intended to improve code quality and security by identifying
adherence to coding standards and detecting potential vulnerabilities.
1. Model: A fine-tuned version of the LLaMA 3 model will be trained to assess
Python code quality and detect security threats.

CHAPTER 3
SYSTEM OVERVIEW
This chapter provides a comprehensive overview of the Reagent plugin system, including
its functionality, context, and design. The Reagent plugin is designed to assist Python
developers by offering real-time detection of PEP8 violations, security vulnerabilities,
and live code suggestions, all within the PyCharm IDE.
3.1 Architectural Design
The Reagent plugin is designed using a client-server architecture to support real-time de-
tection of PEP8 violations, security vulnerabilities, and live code suggestions while main-
taining efficient processing. This modular program structure allows for a clear separation
of concerns between the client-side interface and the backend analysis system.
3.1.1 Modular Program Structure
1. Client-Side (PyCharm IDE with Reagent Plugin) The developer interacts with the
system through the PyCharm IDE. As the developer writes Python code, the Reagent
Plugin sends the code to the backend server for analysis in real-time. The Reagent
Plugin receives analysis results, including PEP8 violations, security vulnerabilities,
and live suggestions, and displays them to the developer immediately.The plugin
also allows the developer to generate a summary report detailing all detected is-
sues.
2. Server-Side (Backend Server) The backend server handles the core analysis logic
and machine learning models responsible for detecting PEP8 violations and security
vulnerabilities. It consists of:
11
3. System Overview
• PEP8 Violation Detector: Detects formatting violations based on PEP8 stan-
dards.
• Security Threat Detector: Identifies potential security vulnerabilities in the
code.
• Live Code Suggestion Engine: Provides real-time suggestions to improve the
code.
• Summary Generator: Compiles a summary report of the analysis results.
3.1.2 Relationships Between Modules
The interaction between the client (Reagent Plugin) and the server is orchestrated as fol-
lows:
• The Developer writes code, which is processed by the Reagent Plugin.
• The plugin sends the code to the backend server for analysis.
• The backend runs checks for PEP8 violations and security threats and returns the
results to the plugin.
• The plugin then displays these results and suggestions to the developer in real-time.
3.1.3 Box and Line Diagram
In the initial design stage, we use a Box and Line Diagram to represent the system’s
structure. Below is the diagram showing the major subsystems and their connections.
Figure 3.1: Box and Line Diagram of Reagent Plugin
12
3.2 Design Models
3.1.4 Final Architecture
The finalized architecture follows a Client-Server Model, where the Reagent Plugin acts
as the client, and the Backend Server performs all code analysis. This structure ensures
efficient processing while allowing real-time interactions for the developer.
The final architecture is detailed as follows:
• Client Side: Developer interface (PyCharm IDE) and the Reagent Plugin.
• Server Side: PEP8 Violation Detector, Security Threat Detector, Live Code Sug-
gestion Engine, and Summary Generator.
This architectural design enables the Reagent plugin to provide seamless feedback to
developers while maintaining modularity for easy future enhancements.
3.2 Design Models
This section presents the design models applicable to the Reagent plugin project. Each
model is critical in understanding the system’s architecture and functionality. The follow-
ing design models have been developed following a procedural approach:
3.2.1 Activity Diagram
The Activity Diagram illustrates the workflow of the Reagent plugin, showing how the
developer interacts with the plugin in real-time. It captures the sequence of activities
from writing code to receiving feedback.

3.3 Data Design
Figure 3.7: Entity-Relationship Diagram for Reagent Plugin
3.2.5.1 Description of Entities and Relationships
The main entities in the ERD are:
• Code File: Represents the Python code being analyzed.
• PEP8 Violation: Represents violations detected based on PEP8 standards.
• Security Threat: Represents potential security vulnerabilities found in the code.
• Live Suggestion: Contains real-time suggestions for improving the code.
• Summary Report: Compiles a summary of all analysis results.
The relationships among these entities indicate that a Code File can have multiple PEP8
Violations and Security Threats, and can generate multiple Live Suggestions and a Sum-
mary Report. This structure is crucial for maintaining an organized approach to code
analysis and improving developer productivity.
3.3 Data Design
3.3.1 Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)
• Purpose: Used for parsing Python code and analyzing its structure.
• Description: The AST is a hierarchical tree structure that represents the syntactical
structure of Python code. It’s useful for performing static analysis, detecting code
smells, and finding PEP8 violations. AST nodes can represent code elements such
as functions, loops, variables, etc.
Example: Analyzing function definitions, loops, and variable assignments to detect
PEP8 violations or vulnerabilities like hard-coded credentials.
17
3. System Overview
• Structure: Tree with nodes representing syntactic constructs in the source code.
3.3.2 Hash Maps (Dictionaries)
• Purpose: Used to store and retrieve key-value pairs efficiently.
• Description: Used to store the mapping between line numbers of code and cor-
responding issues (e.g., PEP8 violations or vulnerabilities). It can store settings
for users, such as preferences or plugin configurations. Example: { line_number:
["PEP8 violation: Missing docstring", "Security flaw: SQL injection"] }
• Structure: Dictionary or HashMap for fast lookups, insertion, and deletion.
• Purpose: Store sequential data such as lines of code, user inputs, or logs.
• Description: Lists are used to hold sequential data, such as the lines of code being
analyzed. Example: A list of all lines in the source code file being analyzed. Each
entry might be indexed by the line number.
• Structure: Simple arrays or dynamic lists (in Python, list).
3.3.3 Tuple (Immutable Data)
• Purpose: Used to store immutable data such as analysis results that do not need to
be modified.
• Description: Tuples can be used to store static or immutable data such as the ini-
tial analysis of code lines (PEP8 violations) or security threat metadata. Example:
(line_number, violation_type, severity)
• Structure: A collection of immutable, ordered elements.
3.3.4 Linked List
• Purpose: Used for log tracking and version control.
• Description: A linked list can help in tracking changes to the code and the analysis
process over time. It is useful for version control features, like undo/redo function-
ality, where each change is stored as a node in a linked list.
• Structure: The single or doubly linked list to track changes.
18
3.3 Data Design
3.3.5 Bloom Filter
• Purpose: Efficiently check for common patterns or known vulnerabilities.
• Description: A Bloom Filter can be used to quickly determine if a certain pattern
or vulnerability signature has already been detected without explicitly storing all the
data. Example: Checking for common vulnerability signatures (e.g., SQL injection
patterns) across large codebases.
• Structure: Probabilistic data structure for efficient membership testing.
3.3.6 Event Queue
• Purpose: Queue for real-time events such as feedback and suggestions.
• Description: Used to manage real-time events like user actions or plugin-triggered
alerts, ensuring smooth interaction without overwhelming the user. Example: An
event queue to handle real-time suggestions, warnings, and logs from the plugin as
the user writes code.
• Structure: FIFO queue for sequential handling of events.
3.3.7 Data Flow
1. Code Ingestion
• Code from PyCharm is input into the plugin.
• AST is generated for structural analysis.
2. Analysis and Storage Issues such as PEP8 violations and vulnerabilities are stored
in HashMaps for fast lookup. The severity of issues is stored in a Priority Queue to
display the most critical errors first.
3. Code Summaries Code summaries and auto-complete suggestions can be stored
and fetched from a Prefix Tree.
4. Log and Error Tracking Errors are stored in a Linked List for easy tracking and
recovery.
5. Training Data Storage Training data is handled through Dictionaries and mapping
code snippets to analyze results during the machine learning phase.
19

CHAPTER 4
CONCLUSION AND FUTURE WORK
4.1 Conclusion
Reagent is a powerful tool that can help novice programmers become professionals by
helping them learn and adapt to write clean, efficient, and secure code. Reagent will use
advanced LLMs (Large Language Models) to detect code standard violations and vulner-
abilities. The plugin will then suggest code fixes via popup messages and alerts to ensure
the code is fixed on the go. Reagent aims to help Python programmers; therefore, it will
provide seamless integration with PyCharm. Reagent’s functionality is underpinned by a
well-thought-out data design that utilizes structures like Abstract Syntax Trees (ASTs) for
code parsing and hash Maps for quick retrieval of analysis results for issue prioritization
and summary generation. These choices ensure the plugin can efficiently handle real-time
operations without compromising performance, even in large codebases. Additionally, the
system’s ability to track changes via Linked Lists and handle real-time events through an
Event Queue ensures smooth user interaction and reliability. The non-functional require-
ments, such as reliability, usability, performance, and security, are addressed through
specific, measurable goals, including low failure rates, intuitive user interfaces, fast re-
sponse times, and robust security measures. Reagent will be capable of processing com-
plex Python codebases while maintaining a low resource footprint, ensuring a seamless
developer experience. Ultimately, Reagent empowers both novice and professional de-
velopers to write high-quality, secure, and efficient Python code. Its real-time feedback,
coupled with deep learning-powered analysis, will significantly enhance productivity and
code quality, bridging the gap between theoretical best practices and their application in
everyday coding tasks.

Chapter 1 
1. Introduction 
The purpose of this project proposal document is to collect, analyze, and define high-level needs 
and features of our proposed app, "VirtuLectra". 
This web-based application is geared towards teachers who want to lessen their burden and 
students who would benefit greatly from interactive lectures. 
 
1.1. Problem Statement 
Current online tutoring platforms often fall short of providing a truly interactive learning 
experience. Teachers face the challenge of managing diverse student needs, creating customized 
content, and maintaining student engagement, all while managing their existing workload. Due to 
this, students struggle with the lack of personalized feedback, and real-time interaction. 
 
6 
 
Table 1 Problem Statement 
 
 
1.2. Scope 
 
The scope of VirtuLectra focuses on delivering a comprehensive AI-powered educational platform 
designed to enhance both teaching and learning experiences. The platform supports real -time 
interaction between the system and students, automated content generation, and student monitoring 
features. By leveraging artificial intelligence, VirtuLectra aims to lessen the heavy workload on 
teachers by automating repetitive tasks like content creation and grading. At the same time, it 
enhances student engagement by enabling real -time feedback. The platform is built to adapt to 
diverse educational needs, offering a dynamic solution. However, the effectiveness of most of these 
features depends on certain limitations, such as the availability of high-quality internet connectivity. 
The problem of Lack of interactive and efficient teaching tools for teachers, leading 
to increased workload and lack of student engagement. 
Affects ●Teachers 
● Students 
The result of which Teachers: 
● Cannot effectively respond to students in real time, causing 
disengagement. 
● Lack of standardized tools to automate content generation, 
increasing preparation time. 
Students: 
● Receive limited real-time feedback, which hampers their ability to 
stay engaged and improve on their learning path. 
Benefits of ● Automated Content Creation : Teachers benefit from AI -driven 
lecture notes and tailored quizzes, saving time on preparation and 
grading. 
● Real-Time Interaction : Students enjoy dynamic interaction, 
ensuring real-time feedback and engagement during live sessions. 
 
7 
 
Limitations 
Dependence on Internet Connectivity : Requires stable internet for real -time interaction and 
content updates. 
Scalability of AI Systems : Handling large numbers of students may require significant 
computational resources, potentially limiting performance in large-scale deployments. 
Subject: Programming Fundamentals (For proof of concept) 
Language: English only 
Content Generation: Mcq-based quizzes only. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
8 
 
1.3. Modules 
 
1.3.1. Real-Time Interaction 
This module facilitates dynamic, real-time engagement between the 
system and students during live lectures through text-to-speech models. Key features 
include: 
a. Conversational Voice Exchanges: Enables natural, interactive 
voice conversations between the system and students, creating a realistic and 
immersive classroom environment. This interaction mimics a live classroom setting. 
b. Real-time Q&A Sessions: Utilizes LLM to address student 
questions when they are asked. This proactive approach helps ensure that students' 
queries are resolved promptly. 
 
1.3.2. Automated Content Creation 
This module streamlines the creation of educational materials using 
LLMs, significantly reducing the teacher’s workload: 
a. Lecture Script and Notes Generation: Automatically generates 
comprehensive lecture script and notes from the course content. These notes include 
key points, summaries, and important details. 
b. Tailored Assessments: Creates customized quizzes aligned with the 
course material. These are designed to evaluate students’ understanding of the 
content. Also automated grading of quizzes for quick feedback. 
 
1.3.3. Student Monitoring 
This module monitors and analyzes student engagement and 
understanding during lectures: 
a. Engagement Tracking: Uses LLM and scheduled pop-up questions 
to track student attention levels and participation during live sessions. This includes 
monitoring student responses, and overall involvement in the lecture. 
 
 
9 
 
1.3.4. Web Portal 
The web portal serves as the user interface for both teachers and students. 
It offers easy navigation and a seamless experience, allowing access to all the features of 
the platform: 
a. Teacher Dashboard: Provides teachers with tools to manage 
lectures, review student progress, and generate content. 
b. Student Interface: Students can access their learning materials, 
quizzes, and real-time lectures via the portal. 
c. Integration with Modules: The portal integrates the Real- Time 
Interaction, Automated Content Creation, and Student Monitoring modules, 
allowing both teachers and students to interact with the AI- powered features 
effectively. 
 
1.4. User Classes and Characteristics 
Demographic: The platform is designed for teachers and students from a wide range of educational 
institutions, including schools, universities, and private tutoring programs. 
User Classes: 
Teachers who want to decrease their burden. 
Students who want more interactive and engaging learning.

the quiz based on the updated settings, providing a new 
preview for review. 
3. Preview Quiz: 
○ Trigger: The teacher wants to review the 
entire quiz before making it available to students. 
○ User Action: The teacher selects the option 
to preview the full quiz. 
○ System Response: The system displays the 
complete quiz for a comprehensive review. 
4. Delete Generated Quiz: 
○ Trigger: The teacher decides to remove a 
generated quiz before publishing. 
 
21 
 
○ User Action: The teacher selects the option 
to delete the quiz. 
○ System Response: The system removes the 
quiz from the generation queue and notifies the teacher of 
the successful deletion. 
Exceptions: 1. Generation Failure: 
○ Trigger: The system fails to generate a quiz 
due to processing errors. 
○ System Response: Displays an error 
message such as "Unable to generate quiz at this time. Please 
try again later.". 
○ User Action: The teacher can attempt to 
regenerate the quiz after addressing the issues or contact 
support for assistance. 
2. Partial Quiz Generation: 
○ Trigger: Some quiz questions fail to 
generate due to content gaps. 
○ System Response: Informs the teacher 
which specific questions cannot be generated and provides 
options to manually create those questions. 
○ User Action: The teacher manually adds the 
missing questions or adjusts the lecture topic parameters to 
facilitate complete quiz generation. 
3. System Timeout: 
○ Trigger: The system takes longer than 
expected to generate the quiz due to large content size or 
server issues. 
○ System Response: Displays a timeout 
notification and offers the teacher the option to retry or 
cancel the quiz generation process. 
○ User Action: The teacher can choose to 
retry the generation process or cancel and address any 
underlying issues. 
 
 
 
22
