# FYP1-MidReport-F24-154-D-CommuneX - i211188 Sheraz Tariq

## 1. Introduction

1. Introduction
1.2 Scope
The Scope of Neighborhood and Community automation system involves the comprehensive
platform that integrate with various modules to improve the community operations. The func-
tionalities involve the user authentication, seamless communication platform, incident report-
ing and community voting system through block chain. Moreover, the property documents will
be handled and created using a blockchain. The blockchain system will provide the safe trans-
action system for the communities and the real time notification. Event management include
managing the events in the community as well as the resource would be shared in the commu-
nities to enhance the automated system. The community will do their payments and transaction
using blockchain. The project aims to enhance communication, efficiency and automate the
community on advance culture or in a global village.
1.3 Modules
1.3.1 Property buying and selling
Description: This module will simplify the processes and procedures of the properties whether
it is the buying or selling, money transactions or just viewing the prices of the plots and houses.
1. Feature 1: Buying and selling
2. Feature 2: Block chain based documentations
1.3.2 Event Management
Description: This module will help the community members to book any sort of events, places
for birthday parties or just a personalized event. Our model will also recommend the events to
the members.
1. Feature 1: Event Booking
2. Feature 2: Event recommendation.
1.3.3 Decentralized voting system
Our platform will have the functionality of a voting system which will let the users to record
their say in the important decisions of the community. The voting process will be smoother and
efficient and will be kept secure.
2
1.3 Modules
1. Feature 1: Block chain based voting system
1.3.4 Shared Resources
Description: This module will enable the residents to share each other resources. The members
of the society will be able to request different services from their fellow neighbors. This will
increase the community interaction and promotes resources efficiency.
1. Feature 1: Offer or Request services from neighbors.
1.3.5 User Authentication and Role Management
Description: This module will ensure a smooth and secure access to our system. Different
functionalities such as sign up/login, password recovery, permissions will be managed.
1. Feature 1: User Registration and login
2. Feature 2: Credentials based on Roles
1.3.6 Communication and Announcement Module
Description: This module will facilitate a well-organized connection between the community
members. Essential information will be conveyed to others and a proper discussion could be
made on announcements.
1. Feature 1: Community news and announcement
2. Feature 2: Private Messaging
1.3.7 Admin Dashboard
Description:The admin dashboard will have access to the advanced tools that will help manage
daily community operations.
1. Feature 1: Comprehensive Overview
2. Feature 2: User Management

2. Project Requirements
2.3 Non-Functional Requirements
2.3.1 Reliability
Failure Rate: The System shall has mean time failure between of minimum 1000 hours.
Failure Definition: The system will be unavailable due to error in any functionality and prop-
erty listing, voting, resource sharing, communication does not operate.
Consequence: Residents will unable to use any operation and result in data loss.
Protection from Failure: The system shall have a back mechanism to prevent it from any
single failures.
Error Detection Strategy The system shall monitor all the logs continuously in case of error
detection.
Error Correction Strategy The system shall have automated backup mechanism to recover
single operation with in 5 minutes.
2.3.2 Usability
Ease of Learning: The system shall designed in a way by which user can learn to use within
10 minutes.
Ease of Use: The system shall has user-friendly interface and it will allow user to done a task
with minimal effort.
Examples
USE-1 The system shall allow the user to retrieve the property listing with a single interaction.
USE-2 The system shall guide a new user step-by-step to buy new property process.
USE-3 The system shall display an error message when a user enter incorrect or invalid details.
2.3.3 Performance
Response Time: The system shall respond to the user requests with in 3 seconds.
Scalability: The system shall handle the user when increases without any degradation in per-
formance.
Load Handling: The system should be able to handle the loads when on its peak number of
users.
Resource Utilization: The system shall use the resources RAM, CPU and GPU if exist in user
system.
Throughput: The system shall be able to do maximum number of transaction.
34
2.3 Non-Functional Requirements
Examples
PER-1: The system shall load 90 percent of all the web pages within the 4 seconds from the
time the user requests the page over 20 Mbps or more fast internet.
PER-2 The system shall be able to handle at least 500 residents without performance degrada-
tion.
PER-3 The system shall be able to handle at least 50 transaction during system peak time.
2.3.4 Security
Data Encryption: The system shall encrypt all sensitive data and secure them in a block chain.
Authentication and Authorization: The system shall use strong authorization and authenti-
cation methods i.e two factor, MFA etc.
Access Control: The system shall allow users to perform activities according to their role.
Audit Trails: The system shall audit all transactions and trail related activities.
Examples
SEC-1: The system shall use standard algorithms to protect the sensitive information.
SEC-2: The system shall have a multi factor authentication or 2FA authentication for the ad-
min login.
SEC-3: The system shall maintain the logs of users and have at least 6 months audit details.
35

Chapter 3
System Overview
3.1 Architectural Design
The CommuneX system is designed to manage several aspects of Neighborhood and Commu-
nity automation which include the buying and selling of the properties, the decentralized voting
system for the community decisions, property documents stored on a block chain to maintain
the security and transparency and resource sharing among the community members. By de-
composing these modules into the system we will be providing the complete web application
for the community purpose. These modules integrates with each other maintain the desired
functionality.
3.1.1 System Architecture Design
The architecture diagram for CommuneX outlines a system that integrates various aspects of
community management. Users interact through a client interface, such as mobile or web
applications, which connect to a backed powered by Node.js. The server processes requests,
manages data flow, and interacts with both a blockchain for secure decentralized operations
(like voting or property transactions) and a database that stores community-related information.
The design ensures secure data handling and efficient management of community services like
communication, voting, and shared resources.

3. System Overview
3.3 Data Design
The community automation system information domain is converted into the data structures
which help us to store the data processing and the entities like properties, voting creation, vot-
ing results, voting, resources, community engagement, private messaging and resource book-
ings. These entities store in non structural SQL like MongoDB while the property transaction,
property papers and the other content stored on block chain. By arranging the data in this way
our system ensure data transparency, security and information protection to improve the user
experience.
The database design for the community automation system will be:
3.3.1 User Management
It stores data of each users including userID, name, email, password, role and the contact
information. The userID assign to the user automatically by the mongoDB.
3.3.2 Property Management
The property management contains the list of the property for sale and the transaction details.
For the property listing the entities are ownerID, location, size, price details, photos, status for
availability.
The transaction details include propertyID, buyerID, sellerID, date of transaction, price and the
status.
3.3.3 Decentralized Voting system
The voting system contains two databases one for the voting creation and other is for casting a
vote.
The voting proposal creation includes votingID, title, description of voting, options for vote,
deadline and status of the voting.
The voting includes proposalID, voterID, voting option and the timestamp for the voting.
3.3.4 Resource Sharing
The resource sharing contains two databases. One is for resource listing and other is for re-
source booking.
The resource booking contains each resource ID, ownerID, type, description, availability, pho-
66
3.3 Data Design
tos and status.
The resource booking includes ID, resourceID, userID, booking period and the status.

1.3 Modules
1.3.1 Module 1 - Codebase Analysis
1. Project Structure Extraction: The tool will go through and analyse the currently
active project to extract the folder structure which will help in extracting informa-
tion. The system will identify all the relevant files (.py files) as well as extract and
structure the content within them.
2. Abstract Syntax Tree Generation: Using the previously structured code, the tool
will generate an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) for each file. The ASTs will provide
a representation of the code, allowing us to perform a in-depth analysis of the code.
3. Code Dependency Graph Generation: The tool analyze the imports and create
a code dependency graph. This graph will show how different files are related
to each other, facilitating the analysis of code smells and visualizing the inter-file
dependencies that exist within the project.
1.3.2 Module 2 - Detection Module
1. Real Time Detection of Code Smells: The tool, upon opening a project, starts a
real-time detection process for code smells. As users make changes to the code and
it becomes compilable, AST analysis is performed on the modified file.
2. User Triggered Detection of Code Smells:In the tool, the detection of certain sub-
set of code smells will meed to be manually triggered by the user. By default, it will
3
1. Introduction
only detect code smells present in the currently opened file. However, if needed,
the user can choose to scan the entire project to find these issues everywhere.
1.3.3 Module 3 - Refactoring Module
1. Refactoring Readiness Assessment of Identified Code Smells: The system will
evaluate whether identified code smells are suitable for automated refactoring by
large language models (LLMs). This feature will include the analysis of various
factors, including code complexity, and dependency relationships, to determine if
an LLM can effectively refactor the code.
2. Refactoring of Identified Code Smells: After detection of code smell, users can
then choose to either manually adjust the code or utilize the automatic refactoring
option to change their code depending on the results of the Refactoring Readiness
analysis.
3. Filter and Refactor specific code based on detected code smells: By filtering
code smells such as long functions, inconsistent naming, and god objects, users can
decide which issues they would like to address first and apply specific refactoring
techniques to improve code quality.
4. Cascading Code Refactoring: The refactoring process ensures that every im-
pacted file within the code base is consistently updated. Any modifications made to
one file will be reflected across the entire code base.
5. Isolated Compilation and Testing of Refactored Code base: Users can com-
pile and run a temporary version of the refactored code base before accepting the
changes, allowing them to validate and test the modifications without altering the
original code base.
6. Isolated Compilation and Testing of Refactored Code (Supplementary): Users
can compile and run a temporary version of the refactored code before accepting
the changes, allowing them to validate and test whether the modifications are se-
mantically same.
7. Personalized Refactoring Suggestions (Supplementary): Refactoring sugges-
tions will be tailored to the user’s coding style and patterns. When a user writes
code, the tool analyzes the user’s coding practices. It then generates refactoring
recommendations that align with these preferences. This personalized approach
ensures that the refactored code remains consistent with the user’s coding practices.
4
1.3 Modules
1.3.4 Module 4 - Change logs Module
1. Code smell detection and Refactoring tracker: This tracks the detection and
refactoring of code smells, while also keeping track the structure and dependencies
between files. It provides a detailed history of how code smells have been managed.
2. File Dependency Tracker: It also tracks the inter-dependencies within the project
that have evolved over time, offering developers a clear understanding of the project’s
progression and the impact of their changes.
3. Revert Readiness Assessment: This feature is to ensure that any refactoring that
the user wants to revert back to his original code is analyzed and determined to be
possible to be reverted without causing error in the code or inconsistency.
4. Code rollback: This feature allows users to rollback certain changes made through
automated refactoring given that the code snippet has passed the revert readiness
assessment.
1.3.5 Module 5 - Rulesets Module
1. VS Code Extension Rulesets:
(a) Rulesets for Detection: Users can specify certain rules that the tool will con-
sider during code smell detection. For example:
i. Include and Exclude Detection of Certain Code Smells for a Repository
ii. Include and Exclude Files for Detection of Code Smells
(b) Rulesets for Refactoring: Users can specify certain rules that the tool will
consider during automated code refactoring. For example:
i. Include and Exclude Refactoring of Certain Code Smells for a Repository
ii. Include and Exclude Files for Refactoring of Code Smells
1.3.6 Module 6 - Website development Module
1. Shareable Logs Access: The user can add viewers to their project. These viewers
will be able to view and analyze the history and evolution of different code smells
over the life cycle of the project. This ensures that the viewers fully grasp the
evolution of code smells within a code base over time and how they were handled
or refactored.
5
1. Introduction
2. Project Visualization: This allows users to view the entire project structure, in-
cluding the dependencies between code files. This interactive visualization will
enable the users to view and analyze the project. This will aid their understanding
of how different components of the project are connected.
3. Inter-File Impact Analysis: The user would be able to view and understand how
modifications in one file would impact other files present in the code base. This
helps them understand the impact of the changes and keep track of how different
files are connected with each other.
4. Code Smells Timeline: The tool will maintain a timeline of identified code smells,
along with specific details about them. It will also track refactored code, allowing
users to view the changes made over time.

2.2 Functional Requirements
2.2.1 Module 1 - Codebase Analysis
1. Project Structure Extraction
1.1.1 The system shall scan the active project folder and subfolders to identify all
.py files.
1.1.2 The system shall only process files with the .py extension for analysis and
content extraction.
1.1.3 The system shall structure the extracted content in a format that allows for
further analysis.
2. Abstract Syntax Tree Generation
22
2.2 Functional Requirements
1.2.1 The system shall generate an AST for each Python file.
1.2.2 The system shall automatically re-generate the AST for a file whenever project
is saved.
1.2.3 The system shall indicate whenever AST generation for a specific file fails.
1.2.4 The system shall restructure the AST to ensure further processing.
3. Code Dependency Graph Generation
1.3.1 The system shall create an inter-file dependency graph by analyzing the code-
base to identify how different files are related to each other.
1.3.3 The system shall update the inter-file dependency graph whenever the AST is
updated.
1.3.4 The system shall represent files as nodes of the dependency graph.
1.3.5 The system shall represent inter-file dependencies as directed edges in the
dependency graph.
1.3.6 The system shall ensure that the graph accurately reflects the current state of
the codebase, including all inter-file connections and dependencies.
2.2.2 Module 2 - Detection Module
1. Real-Time Detection of Code Smells
2.1.1 The system shall scan all the Python files in the workspace for code smells
after regular intervals.
2.1.2 The system shall run detection on all the scanned files for any present code
smells.
i. The system shall detect occurrences of magic numbers within the code-
base.
ii. The system shall detect unutilized variables within the codebase.
iii. The system shall detect occurrences of dead code within the codebase.
iv. The system shall detect functions or methods with long parameter lists.
v. The system shall detect conflicts caused by the use of global variables.
vi. The system shall detect overly complex conditional statements within the
codebase.
vii. The system shall detect occurrences of too many nested loops within the
codebase
viii. The system shall detect occurrences of unreachable code within the code-
base.
23
2. Project Requirements
ix. The system shall detect excessive use of flags within the codebase.
x. The system shall detect violations of variable naming conventions within
the codebase.
xi. The system should detect inconsistent naming within the codebase.
xii. The system should detect non-descriptive method names within the code-
base
2.1.3 The system shall allow the user to view identified code smells.
2. User Triggered Detection of Code Smells
2.2.1 The system shall allow the user to manually trigger the detection of certain
code smells.
i. The system shall allow the user to manually trigger the detection of long
functions.
ii. The system shall allow the user to manually trigger the detection of switch
statement abuser.
iii. The system shall allow the user to manually trigger the detection for tight
coupling.
iv. The system shall allow the user to manually trigger the detection for god
object.
v. The system shall allow the user to manually trigger the detection of in-
consistent naming.
vi. The system shall allow the user to manually trigger the detection of fea-
ture envy.
vii. The system should allow the user to manually trigger the detection of
code fragments that have been commented out.
viii. The system shall allow the user to manually trigger the detection tempo-
rary field.
ix. The system should allow the user to manually trigger the detection over-
encapsulation.
x. The system shall allow the user to manually trigger the detection middle
man.
2.2.2 The system shall allow the user to view the problematic code snippets within
the file.
2.2.3 The system shall enable the user to view details about the issue detected in the
selected problematic code snippet.
24
2.2 Functional Requirements
2.2.3 Module 3 - Refactoring Module
3.1 Refactoring Readiness Assessment of Identified Code Smells
3.1.1 The system shall evaluate the refactoring readiness of the code.
i. The system shall evaluate the complexity of each identified code smell.
ii. The system shall evaluate the dependencies, as well as the impact of
changes associated with each identified code smell.
3.1.2 The system shall use the dependency graph to determine files that could be
affected by refactoring.
3.3 Refactoring of Identified Code Smells
3.2.1 The system shall allow the user to manually refactor the code.
3.2.2 If the code passes the refactoring readiness assessment, then the system shall
allow the user to use the automatic refactoring option to fix their code.
3.2.3 The system shall allow the user to analyze the changes made through refactor-
ing.
3.3 Filter and Refactor Specific Code Based on Detected Code Smells
3.3.1 The system shall allow the user to filter out which code smells to refactor.
3.4 Cascading Code Refactoring
3.4.1 The system shall propagate any changes made during the automated refactor-
ing process.
3.4.2 The system shall ensure consistency in variable and function names as well as
any other relevant code structures across the entire codebase.
3.5 Isolated Compilation and Testing of Refactored Code Base
3.5.1 The system shall allow the user to do a test run of the refactored codebase.
3.5.2 The system shall run the refactored code base in an isolated environment.
3.5.3 The system shall allow the user to validate the functionality of the refactored
code base.
3.6 Isolated Compilation and Testing of Refactored Code Snippet (Supplemen-
tary)
3.6.1 The system shall allow the user to do a test run of the refactored code.
3.6.2 The system shall run the refactored code snippet in an isolated environment.
25
2. Project Requirements
3.6.3 The system shall display the output of both the refactored and the original
code snippets.
3.6.4 The system shall compare the outputs of both the refactored and the original
code snippets.
3.7 Personalized Refactoring Suggestions (Supplementary)
3.7.1 The system shall analyze user coding practices.
3.7.2 The system shall provide refactoring suggestions according to users coding
practices and style.
2.2.4 Module 4 - Change Logs Module
4.1 Code Smell Detection and Refactoring Tracker
4.1.1 The system shall track all the code smells identified in the detection process.
4.1.2 The system shall track all the automated refactors.
4.1.3 The system shall maintain a detailed change logs of detection and refactoring.
4.1.4 The system shall track the current status of each code smell and update it as
changes occur.
4.1.5 The system should allow users to manually update the status of a code smell.
4.1.6 The system shall continuously track the changes in inter-file dependencies
caused by refactoring.
4.1.7 The system shall allow users to generate reports of change logs.
4.2 File Dependency Tracker
4.2.1 The system shall track inter-file dependencies within the project.
4.2.2 The system shall maintain a history of the inter-file dependencies.
4.3 Revert Readiness Assessment
4.3.1 The system shall perform a revert readiness assessment before trying to revert
a refactored code snippet.
4.3.2 The system shall analyze the impact of the revert across the project.
4.3.3 If the user has manually modified the automatically refactored code snippets,
then the system shall prevent reverting code changes.
4.3.5 If no issues are detected., then the system shall allow the user to proceed with
the revert.
26
2.2 Functional Requirements
4.3.6 If a revert is not possible due to problems,then the system shall notify the user.
4.4 Code Rollback
4.4.2 The system shall replace the refactored code with the original code when re-
vert is triggered.
4.4.3 The system shall ensure that all changes are reverted consistently across the
codebase.
4.4.4 The system shall notify the user upon successful completion of revert.
2.2.5 Module 5 - Rulesets Module
5.1 VS Code Extension Rulesets
5.1.1 The system shall allow the user to filter out which code smells to detect.
5.1.2 The system shall allow the user to filter out which files to consider during
detection.
5.1.3 The system shall allow the user to filter out which code smells to refactor
automatically.
5.1.4 The system shall allow the user to filter out which files to refactor automati-
cally.
2.2.6 Module 6 - Website Development Module
6.1 The system shall allow the user to create an account for codeNexus.
6.2 The system shall allow the user to login to their account using username and pass-
word.
6.3 The system shall allow the user to link their account with the vscode extension.
6.4 Shareable Logs Access
6.4.1 The system shall allow project owners to invite viewers to their project.
6.4.2 The system shall allow project owners to view all the viewers of their project.
6.4.3 The system shall allow viewers to view project owners and other viewers of a
project.
6.4.4 The logs shall display detailed information about each identified code smell.
6.4.5 The system shall allow users to filter logs by specific code smells, file loca-
tions, and dates.
27
2. Project Requirements
6.4.6 The system shall maintain records of all refactoring actions taken on code
smells.
6.5 Project Visualization
6.5.1 The system shall allow the user to view all the files within a project.
6.5.2 The system shall allow the user to visualize the folder structure of a project.
6.5.3 The system shall allow the user to visualize the inter-file dependencies present
in a project.
6.5.4 The system shall visualize how multiple files in the codebase are related.
6.6 Inter-File Impact Analysis
6.6.1 The system shall allow the user to view file dependencies by showing how
modifications in methods, classes, or variables in one file may impact other
files in the codebase.
6.7 Code Smells Timeline
6.7.1 The system shall display a history of code smells detected over time.
6.7.2 The system shall display the number of detections made during each scan.
6.7.3 If a code smell has been refactored automatically,then the system shall display
the modified code snippet .
6.7.4 The system should utilize graphs to show how the number of code smells has
evolved in a code base over time.
2.3 Non-Functional Requirements
2.3.1 Reliability
REL-1 The system shall log all failures and errors.
REL-2 The system shall handle unexpected errors without losing user progress or critical
information.
REL-3 Code reversion features shall restore the code to its original state without causing
any additional issues or inconsistencies.
REL-4 Real-time detection shall update as code changes occur, ensuring that the most re-
cent state of the codebase is always analyzed.
REL-5 Logs and change history shall be recorded and retrievable at any time for review.
28
2.3 Non-Functional Requirements
2.3.2 Usability
USE-1 The visual markers and "Problems" tab interface should be easy to interpret and
navigate.
USE-2 The system shall allow customization of code smell detection and refactoring rules
for flexibility based on different project requirements.
USE-3 Real-time detection shall not disrupt the coding experience, and the results should
be displayed as the developer edits code.
2.3.3 Performance
PER 2 Log generation and updates in the database shall occur without slowing down the
active code analysis and refactoring processes.
PER 3 The LLM Service should provide suggestions and assessments in a timely manner.
2.3.4 Security
SEC-1 The system shall restrict unauthorized access to project logs.
SEC-2 The system shall automatically log out users after a one week of inactivity.
SEC-3 The extension shall allow access only to the open and active project folder and shall
not scan files or directories outside the user’s designated workspace.
SEC-4 User actions, including code smell detection and refactoring, shall be logged se-
curely to prevent tampering or data loss.
2.3.5 Scalability
SCA-1 The detection service should be able to handle large files without performance is-
sues.
SCA-2 The system shall handle large codebases with multiple files and folders.
SCA-3 The extension shall manage multiple user-triggered detection requests, maintaining
smooth functionality for both single-file and project-wide analysis.
SCA-4 The Changelogs Service shall scale to accommodate an extensive history of code
changes, maintaining performance in log retrieval and filtering.
29

Chapter 3
System Overview
3.1 Architectural Design
The architecture includes two client interfaces: a VS Code extension and a web applica-
tion, which interact with an API Gateway. The gateway is connected to a load balancer
provided by Amazon AWS, responsible for distributing incoming traffic efficiently. The
API Gateway routes requests to one of four microservices: Code Smell Detection, Refac-
toring, Refactoring Readiness, and Changelogs.
Among these microservices, the Changelogs service requires authentication for access,
while the Code Smell Detection, Refactoring, and Refactoring Readiness services do not
require authentication. However, the Detection and Refactoring microservices have the
capability to interact with the Changelogs microservice to store and retrieve data. For
these interactions, they must pass user authentication to gain access to changelog data.
The Changelogs microservice has access to a MongoDB database to store and manage
changelog information. It acts as the central hub for all data related tasks across the
system.
In addition to these microservices, there is an LLM (Large Language Model) microservice
that performs advanced processing tasks. This microservice uses Amazon SQS to manage
task queues, where Detection and Refactoring services send their tasks. Once a task is
processed by the LLM, the completed response is added to a message queue, which the
API Gateway accesses to send corresponding messages back to the appropriate client
sockets.
To aid its operations, the LLM microservice uses a VectorDB, enabling vector retrieval for
tasks requiring advanced data processing or contextual information. It also uses a cache
to store frequently accessed vectors, improving response times and reducing redundant
data fetching.
31
3. System Overview
Figure 3.1: Architecture of CodeNexus
The architecture efficiently distributes traffic through the AWS load balancer and manages
the routing and processing of various requests through a combination of authentication
checks, microservices, and an task-processing system with the LLM microservice and
Amazon SQS.

3.3 Data Design
The JSON schema defines the structure of how data is stored and managed within a VS
Code extension for Python projects. Each project has its workspace data, tracking folder
structure, file data, inter-file dependencies, and potential code smells. The following key
components are detailed in the schema:
• Project Structure:
– Each project workspace consists of three primary data objects:
* Folder Structure: This object reprsents the complete structure of the
project directory. The FolderStructure includes all files and subfold-
ers, tracking the entire hierarchy of the project’s file system.
* File Data: For each Python file (.py) in the project, information is main-
tained:
· Code and AST: The raw code of the file and its parsed Abstract
Syntax Tree (AST) are stored for analysis and code analysis.
· Functions: The list of functions defined within the file..
56
3.3 Data Design
· Classes: Details of any classes within the file, capturing variables,
methods, and calls.
· Global Variables: Information about all global variables declared in
the file.
· Imports: Data on all imports from other files, such as imported mod-
ules, classes, or functions, including their names and types. This
helps in understanding the dependencies.
* Dependency Graph: A separate object that represents the inter-file de-
pendencies across the project:
· This is a map of FileNodes, where eachFileNode contains the name
of a .py file.
· Each FileNode has a set of DependencyNodes that represent its re-
lationships with other files within the project.
· A DependencyNode keeps track of a list of UtilizedEntities,
which are details about functions, classes, or variables that are either:
· Exported to another file.
· Imported from another file.
· This structure allows tracking of how each file interacts with others
in terms of sharing and utilizing code elements.
• Code Smells:
– The schema includes an object dedicated to tracking and managing code
smells across the project. This data structure supports 10 different types of
code smells, each as an object that stores:
* Line Numbers and Description: The specific parts of the code where
the smells were identified, along with a description of the issue.
* Tracking and Analysis: For each code smell type, attributes are used
to track whether the analysis was successful, any errors that occurred,
and the specific data related to the smell.
– This data is imprtant for assessing code quality and identifying potential areas
of improvement.
• Attributes and Data Types:
– FileNode Object: A separate object that tracks each .py file’s key attributes,
including its name and its code content. It serves as a reference to single files
and helps manage their connections to other files in the project through the
dependency graph.
57
3. System Overview
– DependencyNode: A sub-object that provides the details of a file’s depen-
dency, specifying the UtilizedEntities shared between files and whether
the file imports or exports classes, functions, or variables.
– CodeSmells Object: For each code smell detected, details such asline_numbers,
unique_violations, unused_variables, and potential conflicts are tracked.
The object records success status, errors, and relevant data for detailed anal-
ysis.
• Data Relationships and Integrity:
– The FolderStructure organizes how files and subfolders are layerd within
the project.
– Each file’s File Data allows for parsing and analyzing the Python code,
which is important for features like code navigation, refactoring, and in-depth
code quality assessments.
– The Dependency Graph maintains the relationships between different .py
files, providing a map of inter-file dependencies.
– The Code Smells object is for identifying and tracking issues in the code-
base, allowing users to improve code quality and maintainability.
This schema allows the VS Code extension to give a detailed view of the project’s struc-
ture, code details, dependencies, and code quality, thereby aiding developers in analyzing,
and improving their Python projects.
58

Glossary
Comments as Code Over-reliance on comments to explain what the code does instead
of writing self-explanatory code.. 22
Dead Code Code that is never executed or referenced anywhere in the project.. 22
Duplicated Code Same code segments that are seen in multiple places, making mainte-
nance challenging.. 22
Duplicated Logic Similar Code Segments which perform the same tasks and have the
same outputs.. 22
Excessive Use of Flags Using boolean flags in methods to control behavior instead of
breaking the logic into separate methods or classes.. 33
Feature Envy A class that seems more interested in the methods of another class than its
own.. 22
Global Variables Conflict Local Variables and Global variables with the same names
that will add conflicts in code.. 33
God Object A class that knows too much or does too much, violating the Single Respon-
sibility Principle.. 22
Inappropriate Intimacy Classes or modules that are too dependent on each other, mak-
ing changes difficult.. 22
Inconsistent Naming Variables, methods, or classes with inconsistent naming conven-
tions that make code harder to read.. 33
Long Functions Functions that are excessively long and difficult to read and maintain..
22
Long Parameter List Methods or functions that take too many parameters, making them
hard to understand and maintain.. 22
61
Glossary
Magic Numbers Hard-coded values in the code that lack context or explanation.. 33
Middle Man Classes that have only one purpose of being used for interactions with other
classes.. 22
Non-Descriptive Method Names Methods that do not clearly indicate their purpose or
functionality.. 33
Over-Encapsulation Making everything private when it could be accessed without ex-
posing unnecessary details.. 22
Overly Complex Conditional Statements Long or complicated if statements that are
hard to follow.. 22
Switch Statement Abuser Refers to the excessive use of switch statements, especially
when they become large and complex. Over-relying on them can lead to hard-to-
maintain code and may indicate that better organization using polymorphism or
other design patterns is needed.. 22
Temporary Field Variables or fields in a class that serve only one function and remain
empty half of the time.. 22
Too Many Nested Loops Deeply nested loops that can lead to performance issues and
reduced readability.. 22
Unreachable Code Code that will never be executed. For example, statements after the
return statement will never be executed.. 22
Unutilized Variables Variables that are declared but never used in the code.. 33
Variables Naming Notation Refers to inconsistent or unclear naming conventions for
variables.. 33

Project Requirements

2.1 Use-case/Event Response Table/Storyboarding
The selection of the requirement gathering technique(s) will depend on the type of project.
For instance, the following use cases outline the key interactions:
1. User Registration and Login
Allows users to create an account, log in with their credentials, or reset their pass-
word.
2. Create and View Writing Projects
Writers can create new writing projects and view existing projects.
3. Manage Chapters
Users can create, edit, delete, and rearrange chapters within a project.
4. Manage Notes
Writers can add, edit, and delete notes for specific chapters or the entire project.
5. Collaborative Writing
Multiple users can collaborate on the same project with role-based access and real-
time editing.
6. View and Comment on Published Projects
Readers can view published projects and leave comments or feedback.
7. Manage Account Settings
Users can update their profile, change email or password, and customize their pref-
erences.
5
2. Project Requirements
8. AI-Assisted Grammar Check
Writers can run grammar and spell checks on their projects using an AI-powered
assistant.
9. Manage Characters
Writers can create, edit, and track character profiles within a project.
10. Switch Language (English/Urdu)
Users can toggle between English and Urdu for both the interface and content cre-
ation.
11. Customize Workspace
Writers can change the appearance of their workspace and adjust settings to opti-
mize their writing environment.
12. Export Project (PDF, DOCX, etc.)
Allows users to export their entire project in various formats such as PDF or DOCX.
13. Version Control
Users can track changes, view the history of edits, and restore previous versions of
their project.
14. AI-Assisted Writing Assistant (ChatBot)
Users can interact with an AI chatbot to get suggestions, guidance, or answers to
questions during the writing process.
15. Manage Project (Edit, Delete)
Writers can edit the details of their project or delete it if necessary.
16. View Analytics and Writing Metrics
Provides writers with analytics on word count, session duration, chapter progres-
sion, and more.
17. Publish Completed Project
Allow writers to share their completed work with readers, publishers, or other . so
others can view it, like, or comment on it.
18. Manage Users and Projects (Admin)
Allow Admin to manage users and projects to maintain system order and address
user issues.

1. Writer accesses the customization settings from their project dashboard.
2. Writer selects from various themes and layouts to change the visual appearance of
the workspace.
3. Writer adjusts text size, font style, and color scheme to suit their visual preferences.
4. Writer configures toolbars and panels for easier access to frequently used tools.
5. System applies and saves the customization settings.
6. Writer resumes work with the new settings enhancing their writing experience.
Extensions
• 2a. If the writer selects a theme or layout that is not compatible with certain fea-
tures:
1. System notifies the writer about the incompatibility and suggests alternatives.
• 3a. If there is a request to save a custom theme:
1. System allows the writer to name and save the custom configuration for future
use.
• 5a. If there is an error applying the settings:
1. System displays an error message and asks the writer to retry.
Special Requirements
• None
Technology and Data Variations List
• Customization options might include predefined themes or allow for more granular
adjustments like manual color selection.
• Settings interface could include a preview feature, allowing writers to see changes
in real-time before applying them.
25
2. Project Requirements
Frequency of Occurrence
• Occasionally, as writers set up their environment according to personal preference
and might not change settings frequently after initial setup.

5. System provides the writer with a download link or automatically starts the down-

2.2.13 Use Case 13: Version Control
Scope: VerseCraft
Level: User-Goal
Primary Actor: Writer
27
2. Project Requirements
Stakeholders and Interests
• Writers: Interested in maintaining and tracking revisions to ensure they can revert
or reference past versions.
• Editors: Benefit from accessing different versions to understand the evolution of
the text and provide contextual feedback.
• System Administrators: Ensure that version control mechanisms are efficient and
data integrity is maintained throughout revisions.
Preconditions
• User must be logged in and have an active project with version tracking enabled.
Success Guarantee (Post Conditions)
• Changes to the project are logged and can be reviewed by the user.
• Users can revert to previous versions of the project as needed.
Main Success Scenario
1. Writer makes changes to a project, which are automatically saved as a new version.
2. Writer wants to view the history of edits and accesses the version history feature.
3. System displays a list of all versions with timestamps and summaries of changes.
4. Writer selects a specific version to review the detailed content of that iteration.
5. If needed, writer can choose to revert the project to a selected past version.
6. System restores the project to the chosen version, while still preserving newer ver-
sions for potential future use.
Extensions
• 2a. If the version history is not enabled:
1. System prompts the writer to enable version tracking for future changes.
• 4a. If the writer selects a version with substantial differences:
28
2.2 Detailed Use Cases
1. System may provide a side-by-side comparison to highlight changes.
• 5a. If the writer reverts to a previous version:
1. System asks for confirmation before making the change to avoid accidental
rollbacks.
Special Requirements
• None
Technology and Data Variations List
• Version control might integrate with cloud storage services to enhance data backup
and recovery.
• Implementations could include graphical interfaces to visually compare differences
between versions.
Frequency of Occurrence
• Regular, as tracking changes is a crucial part of managing a dynamic and evolving
writing project.
2.2.14 Use Case 14: AI-Assisted Writing Assistant (ChatBot)
Scope: VerseCraft
Level: User-Goal
Primary Actor: Writer
Stakeholders and Interests
• Writers: Seek real-time assistance and suggestions to enhance their writing quality
and efficiency.
• Editors: Benefit from writers using the assistant to improve initial drafts before
formal review.
• AI Developers: Focus on refining the chatbot’s capabilities to better serve user
needs and provide accurate, contextually relevant advice.
29
2. Project Requirements
Preconditions
• User must be logged in and actively working within a project.
Success Guarantee (Post Conditions)
• Users receive relevant suggestions, guidance, or answers from the AI chatbot.
• Interactions with the chatbot enhance the user’s writing process and project quality.
Main Success Scenario
1. Writer initiates a session with the AI chatbot through their project interface.
2. Writer asks specific questions related to their writing, such as plot development,
character traits, or grammar.
3. AI chatbot analyzes the query and the context provided by the current content of
the project.
4. AI chatbot provides suggestions, tips, or corrections relevant to the writer’s query.
5. Writer evaluates the chatbot’s feedback and decides to implement the advice or
explore further interactions.
6. System logs the interaction to refine future responses and improve the AI model.
Extensions
• 2a. If the query is beyond the AI’s current capabilities:
1. Chatbot admits its limitations and may suggest external resources or the option
to seek human assistance.
• 4a. If the writer disagrees with the suggestions:
1. Writer can request alternative advice or clarify their question for more accu-
rate support.
• 5a. If the writer frequently uses the chatbot effectively:
1. System adjusts to better align with the writer’s style and preferences over time.
30
2.2 Detailed Use Cases
Special Requirements
• None
Technology and Data Variations List
• The chatbot could utilize advanced natural language processing (NLP) technologies
to understand and generate human-like responses.
• Integration with the project’s content management system to allow the chatbot to
access relevant project details and provide contextual advice.
Frequency of Occurrence
• Frequent, as writers often need assistance throughout various stages of the writing
process.
2.2.15 Use Case 15: Manage Project (Edit, Delete)
Scope: VerseCraft
Level: User-Goal
Primary Actor: Writer
Stakeholders and Interests
• Writers: Need flexibility in managing the details and existence of their writing
projects.
• System Administrators: Ensure the integrity and efficient management of project
data.
• Data Backup and Recovery Teams:Concerned with the safe storage and potential
recovery of deleted projects.
Preconditions
• User must be logged in and must have at least one existing project.
31
2. Project Requirements
Success Guarantee (Post Conditions)
• Project details are updated according to the user’s modifications.
• Projects are deleted as requested, with appropriate confirmations and the possibility
of recovery if needed.
Main Success Scenario
1. Writer accesses the project management section within their account.
2. Writer selects a project to edit or delete.
3. For editing, writer modifies details such as project title, description, settings, or
access permissions.
4. System confirms the changes and updates the project accordingly.
5. For deletion, writer confirms their intention to delete the project.
6. System provides a secondary confirmation prompt to prevent accidental deletions.
7. Upon confirmation, system deletes the project and notifies the writer of the success-
ful deletion.
Extensions
• 3a. If the writer attempts to edit a project while another user is also making changes
(in collaborative projects):
1. System manages the concurrent edits or locks the project temporarily to pre-
vent overwrite conflicts.
• 6a. If the writer decides against deletion after reaching the confirmation prompt:
1. System cancels the deletion process and retains the project.
• 7a. If the system offers a recovery option for deleted projects:
1. Writer is informed about the recovery process and duration during which the
project can be restored.
Special Requirements
• None
32
2.2 Detailed Use Cases
Technology and Data Variations List
• Project editing could include more detailed adjustments like role assignments for
collaborative projects or privacy settings.
• Deletion processes might include automatic archival for a set period before perma-
nent deletion to allow for recovery.
Frequency of Occurrence
• Occasional, as project editing is common but deletions are less frequent, typically
occurring when a project is no longer needed or requires reevaluation.

2.3 Functional Requirements
This section describes the functional requirements of the system in a natural language
style. The functional requirements are organized by modules, reflecting the specific fea-
tures of each system component.
2.3.1 Module 1: Workspace Dashboard
Following are the requirements for the Workspace Dashboard module:
1. Chapter Organization and Outline Management:
• Users can manage their chapters separately, focusing on their content.
• Chapters can be reordered using a drag-and-drop feature, allowing users to
restructure their documents.
2. Collaborative Writing:
• Multiple users can collaborate on the same project, with different project roles
and responsibilities.
• Changes will be fully synced in real-time for all collaborators.
3. Content Customization and Personalization:
• Users can customize their project settings, such as defining themes and content
styles, for a more personalized experience.
4. Notes Creation:
• Users can create and organize notes alongside their written content, making
the revision and editing process smoother.
5. Version Control:
38
2.4 Non-Functional Requirements
• Users can manage and track different project versions, allowing them to return
to previous versions or compare changes to maintain content history.
6. Language Options (English/Urdu):
• Users can create content in either English or Urdu, with dashboard elements
changing according to the selected language.
7. Character Development Window:
• Users can create and manage characters, ensuring coherence with project con-
tent.
• Options for character design and integration into the story workflow will be
available.
8. Plot Development Window:
• Users can create and manage main plots and subplots, customizing their de-
sign to fit the story easily.
9. Publishing Options:
• Users can publish their content with different privacy options (e.g., private or
public).
2.3.2 Module 2: AI-Analysis Tools
Following are the requirements for the AI-Analysis Tools module:
1. Fine-tuned AI-Assistant:
• The system will provide an AI language model specifically designed to en-
hance written content by improving flow and coherence.
2. English Grammar Checker:
• A highly accurate grammar-checking tool will identify errors and improve the
overall readability of the content.
2.4 Non-Functional Requirements
This section specifies non-functional requirements, focusing on qualities like reliability,
usability, performance, and security.
39
2. Project Requirements
2.4.1 Reliability
The system must minimize failures and maintain high reliability. Metrics such as Mean
Time Between Failures (MTBF) will be used to measure reliability. Measures should be
in place to detect and correct errors, specifying the consequences of failure and strategies
to mitigate them.
2.4.2 Usability
Usability requirements include ease of learning, ease of use, error avoidance, and recov-
ery. The requirements are aimed at optimizing the user experience and ensuring accessi-
bility for all users. For example:
• USE-1: The system shall allow users to retrieve their previous work with a single
interaction.
2.4.3 Performance
Specific performance requirements will be outlined for various system operations. For
example:
• PER-1: 95% of webpages generated by the system shall load completely within 4
seconds for users with a 20 Mbps or faster Internet connection.
2.4.4 Security
The system must protect against unauthorized access, with security measures quantified
by factors such as effort, skill level, and time required to breach security. The require-
ments should avoid specific implementation details like passwords.
2.5 Domain Model
A representation of the domain model will be created to illustrate the core components,
entities, and their relationships within the system.

Chapter 3
System Overview
This chapter provides a comprehensive overview of the VerseCraft system, detailing its
functionality, context, and design. It outlines the architectural framework, design models,
and data structures that underpin the application, ensuring a robust and scalable solution
for managing the creative writing process.
3.1 Architectural Design
3.1.1 Modular Program Structure
VerseCraft is architected using a Client-Server model, leveraging a Multi-tiered archi-
tecture to ensure scalability, maintainability, and efficient performance. The system is
decomposed into distinct modules, each responsible for specific functionalities, enabling
seamless collaboration and integration.
3.1.1.1 Architecture Layers
1. Presentation Layer (Frontend):
• Technology: React.js
• Responsibilities:
– Renders the user interface.
– Handles user interactions and input.
– Communicates with the backend through API calls.
• Components:
– Workspace Dashboard
43
3. System Overview
– AI-Analysis Tools Interface
– User Authentication Pages
2. Application Layer (Backend):
• Technology: Node.js with Express.js
• Responsibilities:
– Processes business logic.
– Manages API endpoints.
– Handles data validation and processing.
• Components:
– User Management Module
– Project Management Module
– AI Integration Module
3. Data Layer:
• Technology: MongoDB
• Responsibilities:
– Stores and retrieves data.
– Manages database schemas and relationships.
• Components:
– User Data Storage
– Project and Content Storage
– AI Model Data Storage
4. AI Layer:
• Technology: Hugging Face, Flask, PyTorch/TensorFlow
• Responsibilities:
– Provides AI-assisted functionalities.
– Handles grammar checking and writing suggestions.
• Components:
– AI-Assistant API
– Grammar Checker Service
44
3.1 Architectural Design
3.1.1.2 Module Interactions
• Frontend ↔ Backend: The frontend communicates with the backend via RESTful
APIs, sending user inputs and receiving processed data or responses.
• Backend ↔ AI Layer: The backend interfaces with the AI layer to utilize AI-
driven features, such as grammar checking and writing assistance.
• Backend ↔ Data Layer: The backend manages data transactions, ensuring data
integrity and consistency within the MongoDB database.
3.1.1.3 High-Level System Diagram
Figure 3.1: High-Level System Diagram of VerseCraft
3.1.1.4 Detailed Architecture Style: MVC (Model-View-Controller)
• Model: Represents the data structure (MongoDB schemas for Users, Projects,
Chapters, etc.).
• View: The user interface built with React.js.
• Controller: Backend services in Node.js handling requests, processing data, and
interfacing with the AI and Data layers.
This MVC pattern ensures a clear separation of concerns, facilitating easier maintenance
and scalability.
45

3.3 Data Design
3.3.1 Data Transformation and Structures
VerseCraft transforms the creative writing domain into structured data entities, ensuring
efficient storage, retrieval, and processing. The major system entities include USER,
Project, Chapter, Notes, Notification, Version, Admin, AI-Prompt, TextFile, Activ-
ityLogs, Feedback, Theme, Collaborator, and Publishing.
62
3.3 Data Design
3.3.2 Data Entities and Relationships
1. USER:
• Attributes: USERID (Primary Key), Email, Profile, Age, Gender, Password,
Description
• Relationships:
– One-to-Many with Project (a user can have multiple projects)
– One-to-Many with ActivityLogs (a user can have multiple activity logs)
– One-to-Many with Feedback (a user can provide multiple feedback en-
tries)
– Many-to-Many with Project through Collaborator (users can collabo-
rate on multiple projects)
– One-to-Many with Notification (a user can receive multiple notifications)
– One-to-Many with AI-Prompt (a user can have multiple AI interactions)
2. Project:
• Attributes: PROJECTID (Primary Key), Description, AuthorID (Foreign
Key to USER), CreatedDate, Type, Genre, Tags, Title
• Relationships:
– One-to-Many with Chapter (a project can have multiple chapters)
– One-to-Many with Notes (a project can have multiple notes)
– One-to-Many with Version (a project can have multiple versions)
– One-to-One with Publishing (a project has one publishing status)
– Many-to-Many with USER through Collaborator
– One-to-Many with Feedback (a project can have multiple feedback en-
tries)
– One-to-Many with AI-Prompt (AI interactions related to the project)
3. Chapter:
• Attributes: CHAPTERID (Primary Key), PROJECTID (Foreign Key),Description,
AuthorID (Foreign Key to USER), CreatedDate, Type
• Relationships:
– Belongs to a Project
– One-to-Many with Notes (a chapter can have multiple notes)
– One-to-Many with Feedback (a chapter can have multiple feedback en-
tries)
63
3. System Overview
4. Notes:
• Attributes: NOTEID (Primary Key), PROJECTID (Foreign Key),Description,
AuthorID (Foreign Key to USER), CreatedDate, Type
• Relationships:
– Belongs to a Project
– May be associated with a Chapter
5. Notification:
• Attributes: NotificationID (Primary Key), Description, SenderID (For-
eign Key to USER), ReceiverID (Foreign Key to USER), CreatedDate,
Type
• Relationships:
– Sent from a USER to a USER
6. Version:
• Attributes: VersionID (Primary Key), Description, AuthorID (Foreign
Key to USER), CreatedDate, PROJECTID (Foreign Key), ProjectType
• Relationships:
– Belongs to a Project
7. Admin:
• Attributes: AdminID (Primary Key), Name, Email, Password
• Relationships:
– Manages USER accounts and Project content
– Can send Notifications
8. AI-Prompt:
• Attributes: PromptID (Primary Key), Response, Question, CreatedDate,
Type, USERID (Foreign Key)
• Relationships:
– Linked to a USER
– May be associated with a Project or Chapter
9. TextFile:
• Attributes: TextFileID (Primary Key), Content, AuthorID (Foreign Key
to USER), CreatedDate, Type
64
3.3 Data Design
• Relationships:
– May be associated with a Project or Chapter
10. ActivityLogs:
• Attributes: LogID (Primary Key), DateTime, USERID (Foreign Key),ActionPerformed
• Relationships:
– Belongs to a USER
11. Feedback:
• Attributes: FeedbackID (Primary Key), Likes, Comments, CreatedDate,
USERID (Foreign Key), PROJECTID (Foreign Key)
• Relationships:
– Provided by a USER on a Project or Chapter
12. Theme:
• Attributes: ThemeID (Primary Key), Description, ThemeName, ColorScheme,
Type
• Relationships:
– Can be applied by a USER to customize the workspace
13. Collaborator:
• Attributes: CollaborationID (Primary Key), PROJECTID (Foreign Key),
USERID (Foreign Key), Role, CreatedDate
• Relationships:

3.3.3 Database Schema
MongoDB Collections:
• users
• projects
• chapters
• notes
• notifications
• versions
• admins
• ai_prompts
• text_files
• activity_logs
• feedbacks
• themes
• collaborators
• publishings
3.3.4 Storage and Processing
• User Data: Stored in the users collection with secure password hashing and en-
cryption for sensitive information.
• Project Data: Each project document contains references to its chapters, notes,
versions, and collaborators, enabling efficient retrieval and management.
• AI Data: AI interactions are stored in the ai_prompts collection, linked to their
respective users and projects.
• Collaboration Data: Managed through the collaborators collection, facilitating
role-based access and real-time synchronization.
66
3.3 Data Design
• Activity Logs: User activities are recorded in the activity_logs collection for
auditing and monitoring purposes.
• Feedback Data: Stored in the feedbacks collection, linked to projects or chapters,
allowing users to view and respond to feedback.
3.3.5 Data Flow
1. User Interaction: Users interact with the frontend to create or manage projects,
chapters, notes, and other entities.
2. Backend Processing: The backend handles requests, interacts with the database,
and communicates with the AI layer as needed.
3. AI Integration: AI tools analyze content and provide suggestions, which are then
stored and displayed to the user.
4. Data Storage: All entities are persistently stored in MongoDB, ensuring data in-
tegrity and availability.
3.3.6 Security Measures
• Authentication: Implement this using secure tokens for better management of user
sessions.
• Authorization: Role-based access control is to make sure writers/readers can only
access and update authorized projects and data.
• Data Encryption: Data is encrypted both in transit and at rest.
• Regular Backups: Automatically save the database to prevent loss.
• Audit Logging: All user activities are logged for security monitoring and compli-
ance.
3.3.7 Scalability Considerations
• Database Sharding: MongoDB’s sharding capabilities allow for horizontal scaling
as the user base and data volume grow.
• Load Balancing: Distributing incoming requests across multiple server instances
ensures optimal performance and availability.
67
3. System Overview
• Microservices Architecture: Potential future migration to microservices for indi-
vidual modules (e.g., AI services) to enhance scalability and maintainability.

3.3.9 Summary
The architectural design of VerseCraft leverages a comprehensive data model that encom-
passes all aspects of the writing workflow, from user management to project collaboration
and AI integration. The data entities and relationships are meticulously structured to sup-
port the functional requirements outlined in the system’s use cases. The use of MongoDB
collections facilitates flexible and scalable data storage, while robust security measures
ensure data integrity and user privacy. Scalability considerations like database sharding
and load balancing prepare the system for growth, ensuring a reliable and responsive
experience for users. This structured approach lays a solid foundation for developing a
feature-rich, reliable, and user-friendly writing workflow management tool.
68
Chapter 4
Conclusions
This report details the design and implementation of VerseCraft, a comprehensive writing
workflow management tool. VerseCraft addresses common writing challenges such as
disorganization, writer’s block, and maintaining grammatical accuracy through structured
modules supporting project organization, collaborative writing, and automated assistance.
The platform’s modular architecture ensures scalability and maintainability, providing a
robust and flexible foundation for managing writing projects. Detailed use case analysis,
sequence diagrams, and a well-defined database schema guided the development process.
VerseCraft integrates services such as grammar checking and writing suggestions, de-
signed to enhance the writing process and empower writers with the tools they need to
create and manage their work effectively. The platform offers a centralized and organized
approach to writing, aiming to foster a more productive and enjoyable creative experience.

## 1. Introduction

1. Introduction
this area, we will identify the different types of vegetation and the trends of deforestation
over the years. Regarding the identification of the types of vegetation, we will differenti-
ate between trees, bushes, barren land, and crops. We will monitor and frame the different
types of vegetation on a map with the help of satellite and geospatial imagery. We will
obtain our satellite imagery from our external collaborators, Google Earth Engine, Google
Maps API, and PlanetLabs. This data will feature multi-spectral data which will be essen-
tial for the Machine Learning algorithms to be used in our project. The Machine Learning
algorithms we will employ range greatly according to their application in our project. As
an example, for the purpose of tree counting and mapping, we will employ tree counting
algorithms like Random Forest, Dark Forest, etc. These machine-learning algorithms will
help us monitor deforestation trends and map the vegetation types by clustering, counting,
classification, and other methodologies. We hope to effectively complete our project at
least up to the definition in the above-mentioned scope.

required to deliver a comprehensive platform.

This module handles the gathering, storing, and preprocessing of satellite imagery. The
goal is to ensure that the data is clean, normalized, and ready for algorithm designing,
model training, and integration with cloud storage for data management.

models for tree species identification. It manages model training, validation, and integra-
tion with GPU acceleration services for optimal performance.

events, and integrates climate data for contextual analysis.

search functionality for specific regions or tree categories. The app provides an intuitive
platform for users to access key metrics, receive alerts, and manage their preferences, all
within a visually appealing and efficient design.

Chapter 3
System Overview
TreeVerse is an innovative platform that helps track and protect our world’s forests using
cutting-edge satellite technology. By combining satellite imagery with advanced mapping
tools, we provide a comprehensive view of global forest changes, helping conservation-
ists, researchers, and decision-makers make informed choices about forest management.
What TreeVerse Offers:
1. Real-Time Forest Monitoring Using state-of-the-art satellite data, TreeVerse tracks
forest coverage worldwide, distinguishing between trees, shrubs, and grasslands.
This detailed mapping helps identify areas of concern and opportunities for conser-
vation.
2. Deforestation Alert System Our platform quickly detects and reports forest loss,
providing crucial details about affected regions and potential causes. This timely
information enables rapid response to forest threats.
3. Smart Reforestation Planning When forests are lost, TreeVerse doesn’t just re-
port the problem—it offers solutions. Our system suggests region-appropriate tree
species for replanting efforts, maximizing the success of reforestation projects.
4. User-Friendly Interactive Maps Explore global forest trends through our intuitive
mapping interface. Whether you’re a researcher analyzing data or a concerned
citizen learning about forest conservation, TreeVerse makes complex information
accessible and actionable.
5. Tailored Access for Different Users From environmental organizations to govern-
ment agencies, TreeVerse adapts to various user needs. Each user type gets cus-
tomized tools and data access, ensuring everyone can contribute effectively to forest
conservation efforts.
19
3. System Overview
TreeVerse is more than just a monitoring system—it’s a platform for positive change,
empowering users worldwide to understand and protect our vital forest ecosystems.

1 Introduction 
This document outlines the development of a Predictive System for Commercial Shipping 
Operations. The system will analyze historical datasets to provide trends and predictive insights that 
will assist in decision -making processes relat ing to deployment frequencies, operational statuses, 
maintenance activities, and other factors critical to managing large commercial shipping fleets. The 
system will leverage machine learning models, the system will deliver forecasts that help optimize fleet 
management, improve operational efficiency, and reduce costs. 
The document is intended for several types of readers: 
• Developers: For them to understand the technical specifications and implementation details of 
the system. 
• Project Managers: For them to track the project’s progress and ensure that the defined scope 
aligns with business needs. 
• Marketing Staff: From them to identify key benefits and marketable features of the system. 
• Testers: From them to evaluate the system's functionality, accuracy, and performance. 
• Documentation Writers : To create user manuals and technical documents based on the 
provided requirements and features. 
 
1.1 Problem Statement 
Fleet operations management in commercial shipping poses a big challenge for efficient 
management. In the commercial shipping industry, fleet managers often struggle to predict when 
vessels should be deployed, maintained, or just kept running to avoid unplanned downtime, inadequate 
resource allocation, and increase in costs relating to operational expenses. In the absence of predictive 
tools, fleet managers and operators rely too much on manual oversight which is based on too much 
guessing and implies an error-prone approach that makes little use of the mountains of historical data 
available to improve the decisions being made at all levels. 
The proposed system addresses these challenges by utilizing historical datasets from commercial 
shipping operations. This system analyzes trends of vessel deployment and maintenance activities and 
operational statuses with generation of predictive insights for the fleet managers. These insights will 
allow fleet managers to anticipate future needs, optimize resource allocation, and prevent unexpected 
disruptions. Thus, by reducing uncertainty, the system aims to streamline operations and improve 
overall fleet efficiency. 
6 
 
1.2 Scope 
The Predictive System for Commercial Shipping Operations is designed to provide data -driven 
insights into fleet management by analyzing historical datasets. The system’s scope encompasses 
several key functionalities: 
• Data Collection and Integration : The system collects historical data from various sources, 
including AIS (Automatic Identification System), radar logs, and maintenance records. This 
data is then preprocessed, cleaned, and structured for analysis. 
• Pattern Recognition and Correlation Analysis : The system identifies patterns and 
correlations within the data, such as the frequency of deployments, correlations between 
different vessel types, and operational trends. This helps improve decision-making capabilities. 
• Predictive Analytics: The system forecasts various operational metrics, including deployment 
patterns, future operational statuses, expected maintenance schedules, and potential surges in 
vessel deployments. These predictions enable more effective fleet management and help 
prevent costly disruptions. 
• User Interface and Visualization : The system offers an intuitive, web -based user interface 
that presents predictive insights through reports, alerts, and visualizations. Fleet managers can 
easily access the system’s recommendations, allowing them to take informed actions. 
• System Testing and Evaluation : The system will be tested extensively using historical 
datasets to ensure that its predictions are accurate and actionable. Testing will confirm the 
system's ability to support the fleet management process effectively. 
The scope of this project is limited to providing a proof -of-concept system that demonstrates the 
feasibility and potential benefits of predictive analytics in commercial shipping. It will not include live 
integration with existing operational systems but will lay the groundwork for future expansion. 
 
1.3 Modules/Action Plan 
The system will be based on a modular approach where there will be incremental development as well 
as the testing of critical features during the design phase. Every module is a different functionality that 
is going to get integrated to form the overall working of the system: 
2.2.1 Data Integration and Preprocessing Module 
• Description: This module will ingest historical data from many sources like AIS, Radar, and 
maintenance logs. The ingested data will be cleaned, normalized, and structured so that it is 
prepared for analysis. 
• Key Tasks: 
o Develop methods for data ingestion and preprocessing. 
o Data cleaning techniques to handle missing or inconsistent data. 
o Unified structure for all incoming data to facilitate efficient processing. 
7 
 
2.2.2 Pattern Recognition Module 
• Description: This module applies machine learning algorithms to analyze historical data to 
identify any dominant patterns of vessel deployment, maintenance, and operational statuses. 
• Key Tasks: 
o Implement clustering and classification algorithms to group similar vessel types and 
their behaviors. 
o Identify the patterns regarding deployment based on historical data to make potential 
predictions on future trends. 
o Draw visualizations on typical trends in the commercial shipping space. 
2.2.3 Predictive Analytics Module 
• Description: The predictive analytics module employs advanced algorithms to build the future 
release of the vessel, maintenance requirements, and operational status from previous history. 
• Key Tasks: 
o Design predictive models for predicting future call backs. 
o Create maintenance forecasting models to predict upcoming maintenance schedules. 
o Integrate predictive real -time updates and responses on upcoming trends for fleet 
managers. 
2.2.4 Anomaly Detection Module 
• Description: This module will involve anomaly detection of operational patterns, such as 
sudden increases in maintenance needs or unexplained changes in the vessel's behavior. 
• Key Tasks: 
o Implement algorithms to flag anomalies in operational and maintenance patterns. 
o Design an alert mechanism to communicate the message to the operators when unusual 
behavior is detected. 
2.2.5 User Interface and Visualization Module 
• Description: The user interface of the system will allow the fleet managers and operators to 
visualize the predictive insights generated by the system. 
• Key Tasks: 
o Design and develop a web interface that renders reports, predictions, alerts, etc. 
o Incorporate interactive visualizations like charts, graphs, and maps. 
o Develop a notification and alert management system to provide actionable insights. 
2.2.6 System Integration and Testing Module 
• Description: This module shall cover all the above modules and will integrate all as one system 
and performs detailed testing for the validation of accuracy and performance of the system. 
8 
 
• Key Tasks: 
o Develop integration strategies for combining data, predictive models, and the user 
interface. 
o Create detailed test plans to evaluate system accuracy using historical datasets. 
o Conduct performance testing and optimize system components where needed.

2.2 Functional Requirements 
2.2.1 Module 1: Data Integration and Preprocessing Module: 
• The system shall collect historical data from multiple sources, including AIS (Automatic 
Identification System) logs, radar data, and maintenance logs. 
• The system shall be able to clean and preprocess the collected data to handle missing, 
incomplete, or erroneous data points. 
• The system will integrate and unify the data into a single structured format for efficient 
analysis. 
2.2.2 Module 2: Pattern Recognition and Correlation Analysis Module: 
• The system shall identify patterns in deployment frequencies based on historical data, 
including peak deployment times and vessel type correlations. 
• The system shall recognize correlations between operational statuses at various levels (e.g., 
unit, fleet) and external factors such as weather or sea conditions. 
• The system shall visualize recognized patterns to assist decision-makers in understanding 
trends in fleet behavior. 
2.2.3 Module 3: Predictive Analytics Module: 
• The system shall develop predictive models to forecast future deployment patterns, 
including probable future surges in deployment frequency. 
• The system will provide predictive insights on the number of assets that will be deployed 
based on vessel type, operational status, and historical usage trends. 
• The system will forecast upcoming maintenance needs based on historical maintenance 
logs and external conditions such as weather. 
2.2.4 Module 4: Anomaly Detection Module: 
• It shall alert fleet managers in advance of upcoming required maintenance to prevent 
unexpected vessel downtime. 
• The system shall identify and flag anomalies in vessel operation, maintenance schedules, 
or deployment patterns. 
• The system shall notify fleet managers of any anomalies detected in vessel patterns, 
operational statuses, or maintenance schedules. 
2.2.5 Module 5: User Interface and Visualization Module: 
• The system shall provide a web-based interface for easy access to predictive analytics 
and historical trends. 
• The system shall generate graphical reports (e.g., charts, graphs, heatmaps) that clearly 
depict predictive insights such as deployment trends, maintenance schedules, and 
operational risks. 
26 
 
• Users will be able to interact with the system through filters (e.g., by vessel type, time 
range) to customize their view of the data. 
2.2.6 Module 6: System Integration and Testing Module: 
• The system shall provide a testing module that allows for the validation of predictive 
models against historical datasets. 
• The system will report on prediction accuracy, error rates, and thresholds to ensure the 
system's predictions meet practical operational needs. 
• The system will provide detailed reports on system performance, accuracy, and areas of 
improvement. 
 
2.3 Non-Functional Requirements 
1. Scalability: 
• Must handle large datasets and scale to accommodate multiple fleets data. 
2. Performance: 
• Near-time or In-time data processing with response times of less than 10 seconds. 
3. Reliability and Availability: 
• 99.9% system availability with fault tolerance and reliable backup mechanisms. 
4. Usability: 
• User-friendly interface requiring minimal training, with clear tutorials and 
documentation. 
5. Accuracy: 
• Predictive models should achieve an accuracy rate of 80% or higher. 
6. Maintainability: 
• Easy to maintain and update, with clear documentation and standard development 
practices. 
7. Data Integrity: 
• Ensure accurate and consistent data processing without duplication or corruption. 
8. Extensibility: 
• Flexible architecture to allow future enhancements and integrations.
