## 1. Introduction

1. Introduction
needed to identify and counter cyber threats. Teenagers and young adults are more sus-
ceptible to cybercrimes, such as ransomware attacks and social engineering attacks, in
underdeveloped areas, such as South Asia, which encompasses the Indo-Pak region.
Financial loss, identity theft, and other negative outcomes are possible outcomes of these
attacks. As a result, people could participate in unsafe online activities or become the
targets of cyberattacks, putting others and institutional data at risk.
Existing methods for spreading cybersecurity awareness, including traditional educational
programs and awareness campaigns, often fail to engage audiences effectively. These
approaches tend to be uninteresting and lack the interactivity needed to capture attention,
resulting in a lack of motivation among people to learn and apply essential cybersecurity
practices. As a result, many individuals are reluctant to engage with these resources,
leaving significant gaps in their understanding and preparedness against cyber threats.
1.2 Scope
Project Focus: A game-based intervention for cybersecurity awareness using Unity 3D
and virtual reality.
Target Audience: Teenagers and young adults.
Objective: Design, develop, and implement a 3D game to increase knowledge of cy-
bersecurity threats and best practices in order to increase knowledge and awareness of
cybersecurity threats and best practices in a realistic and engaging setting.
Learning Environment: To make learning relatable and interesting, the game will include
a virtual environment that mimics real-world locations including homes, restaurants, and
institutions. It will include virtual labs, classrooms, and scenarios that demonstrate these
attacks and teach preventive measures, while also providing solutions after falling prey to a
cyber attack. The scope consists of both the technical and educational aspects of the game.
Educational Scenarios: Demonstrates various cyber attacks through interactive scenarios.
Teaches preventive measures against cyber threats. Provides solutions for players after
experiencing cyber attacks.
Social Life Storyline: The game features a realistic storyline that captures the player’s
daily activities. Activities include getting on a bus, attending university, visiting restaurants,
and other daily routines.
• Player Role and Interaction: The player enters the game as a character with
established relationships with various non-player characters (NPCs) based on the
story. Interactions occur with various non-player characters (NPCs) based on the
storyline.
2
1.2 Scope
• Reputation System: The player develops a positive reputation through interactions
with NPCs. Positive social interactions boost the player’s reputation in front of
others.
• Rivals and Attackers: The player faces rivals, such as fellow students, who seek to
embarrass them. Multiple attackers appear as NPCs in different scenarios, adding
challenges to the game.
• Mood Bar System: The game features a ’mood’ bar with two moods: ’happy’ and
’dead inside’. Positive social interactions increase the ’happy’ mood, earning the
player coins. Negative social points shift the mood towards ’dead inside’; if fully
’dead inside’, the player loses that level.
• Reputation Impact: The player’s reputation is influenced by their good or bad
decisions. Good decisions enhance reputation and social points, while bad decisions
and cyber attacks lead to negative outcomes, impacting how NPCs perceive the
player such as leaked personal information.
• Gameplay Progression: The player navigates daily life scenarios as a teenager or
young adult. Scenarios include attending university, visiting restaurants, and other
daily activities. The game spans through all levels of ’University life’ and concludes
on the first day of the player’s office life.
• VR Integration: The game uses VR technology to simulate real-life scenarios.
Features include decision-making that affects scenario selection, virtual social inter-
actions, and real-time feedback. Designed for deployment on various VR platforms,
including Oculus headsets.
• Cyber Attack Taxonomy: The game includes common cyber attacks within its
system boundary such as Phishing, Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) attacks, Brute force
attacks, Malware, Ransomware, Email spoofing, USB-based attacks, Sniffing, De-
fault password exploitation, Spam attacks, Social engineering. These attacks are
implemented in daily life scenarios to educate players on their nature, prevention,
and solutions.
• Out-of-Scope Attacks: Certain attacks are not covered as they fall outside the
core objectives of the project such as DDoS, Clickjacking, Hash injection, ARP
poisoning,DNS attacks, SQL injection. These attacks are considered irrelevant to
the project scope residing in the irrelevant domain of the project.
• Platform Support: The game is optimized for VR platforms, specifically including
Oculus VR headsets.
• Graphics: Graphics include realistic and immersive 3D models which accurately
reflect daily life environments.
3
1. Introduction
• User Interface: Includes simple and intuitive design, feedback mechanisms, easy
navigation and clear suggestions for player assistance.
• Audio: Includes ambient sounds that reflect the game environment and effects that
highlight key events and decisions.
Key functionalities within the project scope include:
• Scenario Simulation: It is the process of modeling actual cyberattacks, including
ransomware, social engineering, and phishing.
• Educational Content: Interactive tutorials and feedback systems to reinforce learn-
ing.
• VR Integration: Support for various VR platforms, including Oculus Quest 2, to
provide an immersive experience.
Localized Content: Scenarios and narratives tailored to the cultural context of the Indo-
Pak region.
1.3 Modules
The game consists of several key modules, each focusing on different aspects of game-
play, from simulating cyber attacks to integrating VR technology. Below is the detailed
description of each module, highlighting its main features.
1.3.1 Module 1: Game Mechanics
This module focuses on the core gameplay mechanics that define how players interact with
the game environment. It includes the basic movement, actions, and interactions available
to the player, ensuring a responsive and engaging gameplay experience.
1. Feature 1: Player Movement and Controls
(a) Develop intuitive player controls for movement, such as walking, running,
working, and jumping, optimized for both standard and VR gameplay.
(b) Implement player actions like interacting with objects, allowing players to
actively engage with scenarios and complete tasks.
2. Feature 2: Interaction with Game Objects
4
1.3 Modules
(a) Provide mechanisms for interacting with objects within the game and non-
player characters (NPCs) to assist with task fulfillment and scenario-specific
event triggering.
(b) Incorporate gameplay elements that are directly related to the game’s cyberse-
curity themes, such as picking up things and hacking terminals.
1.3.2 Module 2: Game Elements
This module covers the game’s visual and aesthetic components, such as the construction
of the environments, characters, and objects. It seeks to define the game’s aesthetic, making
it aesthetically pleasing and thematically coherent.
1. Feature 1: Visual Design of Environments
(a) Build realistic 3D models of buildings, interiors, and other structures to produce
immersive experiences that mimic real-world locations like residences, public
spaces, and university campuses.
(b) Focus on realistic textures, lighting, and environmental effects to enhance the
game’s overall aesthetic and make the settings feel authentic and engaging.
2. Feature 2: Aesthetics and Game Objects
(a) Design game objects, such as furniture, personal items, and digital devices,
with a realistic and culturally relevant style.
(b) Ensure that these elements contribute to the game’s visual storytelling, support-
ing the narrative and the player’s immersion in each scenario.
1.3.3 Module 3: Assets Creation (Environment and Characters
The visual assets of the game, such as the environments, props, and characters, are designed
and created in this module. Its main goal is to produce an immersive visual experience that
closely resembles actual environments.
1. Feature 1: Development of characters
(a) This involves creating characters, such as rivals, allies, and friendly NPCs, with
unique traits, lighting, and visual effects that correspond to their roles in the
game.
2. Feature 2: Development of Immersive Environments
5
1. Introduction
(a) Creating intricate settings, such as public areas, homes, and campuses of
universities, with accurate lighting and textures to improve immersion.
1.3.4 Module 4: Game Scenarios
This module involves the development of storylines, levels, and narrative elements that
guide the player through the game. It includes the design of scenario-based interactions
and educational narratives that teach cybersecurity concepts through engaging gameplay.
1. Feature 1: Storyboards and Narrative Building
(a) Make thorough storyboards for every scenario that describe the story’s develop-
ment and the player’s travel through a variety of real-world locations, including
houses, restaurants, and universities.
(b) Develop narratives that incorporate cybersecurity threats, personal challenges,
and social pressures, aligning with the game’s goals of education and aware-
ness.
2. Feature 2: Scenario Integration and Milestones
(a) Design scenarios that reflect daily life situations, where players face cybersecu-
rity challenges like phishing, social engineering, and other attacks.
(b) Include milestones and tasks that are contextually relevant to the player’s
environment, such as securing personal devices at home, protecting data at
university, or safely interacting in public spaces.
1.3.5 Module 5: UI/UX
This module focuses on the design of the user interface and the overall user experience, en-
suring that the game is intuitive and accessible. It includes the development of storyboards
that outline game flow and visual design, enhancing player engagement.
1. Feature 1: Creation of Game Flow Objects
(a) Map out the design of ‘mood bar’, coins etc.
2. Feature 2: Design Intuitive Menus and In-Game Prompts
(a) Development of clear and start, pause or setting menus, prompts, and feedback
systems that guide the player, making navigation and learning straightforward.
6
1.4 User Classes and Characteristics
1.3.6 Module 6: VR Integration
This module focuses on the virtual reality components of the game, ensuring that all
features are optimized for an immersive VR experience. It enhances player engagement by
simulating realistic environments and interactions through VR technology.
1. Feature 1:Create VR-Ready Controls and Interactions
(a) Development of controls and interaction methods that are tailored for VR
platforms, including support for Oculus Quest 2, allowing players to fully
immerse in the game.
2. Feature 2: Optimizing of 3D Assets and Mechanics for VR
(a) Ensures that the game’s 3D models, environments, and mechanics are optimized
for performance and realism in VR, providing a seamless and engaging player
experience.
1.4 User Classes and Characteristics
Identify the various user classes that you anticipate will use this product, and describe
their pertinent characteristics.
7
1. Introduction
User class Description
Students Students are the game’s primary users. These users will be university students,
young people, or even teenagers who must be aware of the cyber security
threats they may encounter in virtual environments that mimic real-world
locations, including homes, restaurants, and institutions. The age range of
players may be 16 to 25. Common cybersecurity problems that students will
face include social engineering, USB-based attacks, phishing scams, and
more. Decision-making mechanisms and interactive storytelling are all part
of the game’s strategy to engage this demographic.
Faculty
Members
Cybersecurity professionals or experts might use the game for training pur-
poses or to educate young individuals on cybersecurity best practices. Their
role is to evaluate the effectiveness of the game in teaching cybersecurity
concepts and may provide insights for improvements. They may use the game
to simulate real-world cybersecurity incidents for academic or professional
training purposes.
Cybersecurity
Experts
Experts and professionals in cybersecurity can utilize the game to teach or
train youth on cybersecurity best practices. Their role is to evaluate how
well the game teaches cybersecurity principles and provide feedback for
improvement. For academic or professional training objectives, real-world
cybersecurity situations can be replicated in the game.
Additionally, the scenarios can be examined by cybersecurity specialists to
see if they accurately depict cyberattacks that occur in real life. To make sure
that each scenario accurately represents contemporary cybersecurity threats
and strategies, they will assess each scenario’s authenticity. Experts will
also offer suggestions for improvements to guarantee that the game teaches
players about appropriate defensive tactics and the consequences of security
flaws. Their input will help to improve the scenarios, making them more
accurate and consistent with best practices in cyber security awareness.
Game Ad-
ministrator
Game administrators are responsible for maintaining the game, updating
scenarios, and ensuring the VR platform functions properly. They make sure
the VR equipment operates properly and that the game is updated with the
newest cybersecurity risks. Administrators are in charge of user data, system
performance, and game settings.
Example: User Classes and Characteristics

This section outlines the functional requirements of the cybersecurity awareness game,
focusing on features, player interactions, and game mechanics.

The following requirements focus on the visual and interactive aspects of the game:

The system shall provide a simple, intuitive, and consistent user interface for navi-
gating menus, settings, and indirect story-based tutorials.
2. FR2
The game shall offer real-time feedback in response to player actions, displayed
visually on the screen.
3. FR3
The game shall allow the player to view the basic information about his overall

2. Project Requirements
performance, level, and scores.

status using two states: "happy" and "dead inside."

The system shall guide players through challenges by providing contextual prompts
and suggestions.

Chapter 3
System Overview
The Cybersecurity Awareness Game is an innovative educational game aimed at teenagers
and young adults, designed to raise awareness about cybersecurity threats and promote
safe online practices. The game harnesses the power of virtual reality (VR) technology to
create an immersive experience that mimics real-life situations, allowing players to engage
with various scenarios they might encounter in their everyday lives—such as interacting
on social media, attending classes, or navigating online communications.
Functionally, the game integrates a user-friendly interface that guides players through
different levels while providing real-time feedback based on their actions. Players navigate
through challenges that simulate various cybersecurity threats, such as phishing attacks,
malware, and social engineering, learning preventive measures and appropriate responses
in the process. A dynamic reputation system tracks the player’s decisions, affecting their
in-game reputation and social standing, thereby reinforcing the impact of their choices on
their digital lives.
Contextually, the game is developed to resonate with daily experiences of its target audi-
ence, making the learning experience relatable and engaging. The design prioritises both
fun and functionality, ensuring that players are not only entertained but also educated about
essential cybersecurity principles in a safe, interactive environment. This project seeks
to address the growing need for effective cybersecurity education among young users,
equipping them with the knowledge and skills to navigate the digital world confidently and
securely.

3.3 Data Design
(b) Scenario Encounter: The player faces a situation where they are potentially

can avoid or fall victim to the attack.

now more aware of potential threats.

3.3 Data Design
The "Game-Based Intervention for Cyber Security Awareness" system is organised, stored,
and processed. The system uses Firebase, a NoSQL database, to manage player data,
scenarios, decisions, and other game-related entities.
3.3.1 Data Storage Solution
The system uses Firebase (NoSQL) for data storage. Firebase is perfect for handling
real-time data synchronisation, managing complex relationships, and providing scalability,
which is crucial for a VR-based game. Additionally, Firebase supports user authentication,
making it easier to manage player profiles and ensure secure access.
47
3. System Overview
3.3.2 Data Entities and Structure
The major data entities in the system include UserProfile, Scenario, UserDemographics,
and DecisionLog. Each entity is stored in Firebase in a structured and scalable manner.
Below is a breakdown of each entity and its corresponding fields:
1. UserDemographics
At the start of the game, the player will provide their username and gender. This
entity stores basic information about the player.
• userID (string): Unique identifier for each player (auto-generated by Firebase).
• username (string): The player’s username or player name.
• gender (string): The player’s gender (e.g., "Male," "Female," "Other"). This
data helps personalise the player’s experience and can be used to tailor game
content or display gender-specific avatars and preferences.
2. UserProfile
This entity stores player-specific information, including their progress, score, reputa-
tion in the game and profile data.
• userID (string): Unique identifier for each player (UserDemographics).
• username (string): The player’s username or player name.
• cyberSecurityScore (int): Player’s cumulative score based on their decisions
and progress in the game.
• progress (object): Tracks the current level, completed scenarios, and reputation
status.
– currentLevel (int): The level the player is currently on.
– reputationScore (int): Player’s reputation score affected by their decisions
in-game.
– mood (string): Current mood (e.g., "happy" or "dead inside").
– completedScenarios (array of strings): List of scenario IDs that the player
has completed.
• achievements (array of objects): Stores special achievements unlocked by the
player.
3. Scenario
This entity defines the various scenarios in the game, including the type of cyber
attack involved and the possible outcomes based on player decisions.
• scenarioID (string): Unique identifier for each scenario.
48
3.3 Data Design
• scenarioTitle (string): The title or name of the scenario (e.g., "Phishing Attack
at University").
• description (string): Detailed description of the scenario.
• threatType (string): Type of cyber threat demonstrated in the scenario (e.g.,
Phishing, Malware).
• possibleOutcomes (array of objects): Different possible results based on player
decisions.
– outcomeID (string): Unique ID for each outcome.
– decisionRequired (string): The decision that the player must make (e.g.,
"Open suspicious email").
– result (string): The result of the decision (e.g., "Player’s personal data
gets leaked").
– reputationImpact (int): The impact of the decision on the player’s repu-
tation score.
– moodImpact (int): How the decision affects the player’s mood (positive
or negative).
– cyberSecurityKnowledge (string): Educational information provided
after the outcome is reached.
4. DecisionLog
The DecisionLog stores records of the player’s decisions and the corresponding
outcomes. This information helps track the player’s learning journey and game
progression.
• logID (string): Unique identifier for the decision log entry.
• userID (string): ID of the player who made the decision (links to UserProfile).
• scenarioID (string): ID of the scenario being played (links to Scenario).
• decision (string): The decision made by the player.
• outcome (string): The resulting outcome of the decision.
• timeStamp (datetime): The timestamp when the decision was made.
3.3.3 Database Structure in Firebase
The Firebase database is organised into collections and documents. Each entity is repre-
sented as a collection of documents, making it easy to query and store data for each player,
scenario, and decision. Example Firebase structure:
49

3.3 Data Design
3.3.4 Data Processing and Organization
• Firebase Collections: Each entity is represented as a collection in Firebase (e.g.,
UserDemographics, UserProfile, Scenario, DecisionLog). Firebase’s NoSQL struc-
ture allows for flexible, dynamic data storage and efficient querying.
• Player Onboarding: The UserDemographics collection stores the username and
gender provided by players during the onboarding process. This data is linked to the
player’s UserProfile, tracking their progress, score, and reputation throughout the
game.
• Player Progress: Each player’s progress, including completed scenarios, deci-
sions, and learning outcomes, is logged in their profile in the UserProfile collection,
providing a personalised gaming experience.
• Scenario Tracking: Each scenario is stored in the scenario collection, and the
outcomes are dynamically loaded based on the player’s decisions.
• Real-time Updates : Firebase ensures real-time syncing for continuous player
progress updates and immediate logging of scenario outcomes.
• Decision Logs: The DecisionLog collection records each decision made by the
player, enabling tracking of their learning journey and allowing for future analysis.
• Scenario Execution: When a player interacts with a scenario, the system loads data
from the scenario collection. After the player makes a decision, the corresponding
outcome is stored in the DecisionLog, and the player’s UserProfile is updated with
the new progress and reputation score.
This data design is optimised to ensure a smooth and personalised experience for
each player in the VR game, with a focus on scalability, performance, and real-time
interaction.

# FYP1-MidReport-F24-181-D-Little Learner - i210600 Farhan Ahmad

## 1. Introduction

1. Introduction
backgrounds negatively influence both learning outcomes and the quality of interactions.
This requires that there is a need for a more sophisticated approach to teaching in order
to account for regional accents while still creating engaging and personalized lessons to
ensure basic language and number skills development. [22]
These concerns is where it is believed that the proposed solution, that is bearing the name
‘Little Learner’, which will act as an AI based academic tutor, will help by providing
engaging techniques of learning through advanced strategies of machine learning and
speech recognition. This platform would help to narrow this gap and improve preschool
education in each socioeconomic stratum of the country’s population by a correspond-
ingly addressing each stratum’s requirements and speech habits. [33]
1.2 Scope
The scope of the project is the development of our app Little Learner, the AI Tutor that is
specified for children aged between 3-7 [55]. The Tutor will:
• Target Audience: Focus on children aged 3-7, including those with disabilities,
ensuring the system is inclusive and accessible to children with various communi-
cation challenges.
• Technological Focus: Utilize advanced AI technologies, including adapting and
fine-tuning and training current technologies to accurately understand and interact
with children in their native languages (Urdu). The project will create a custom
dataset tailored to the linguistic and accent variations found in children from Pak-
istan.
• Educational Goals: Enhance the educational opportunities for young children by
improving foundational language skills through personalized AI-driven interac-
tions. The project aims to make learning more accessible and engaging, thereby
fostering greater inclusivity in early childhood education.
• Model Development: Fine-tune existing transcription models, followed by devel-
opment of a prompted intent classification model. This will accurately interpret and
respond to children’s voice inputs.
• Data Collection and Processing : Creating a custom dataset of Pakistani chil-
dren(aged 3-7 years old), speaking numbers 0-9. This will include data annotation,
pre-processing and structuring of the data.
• Implementation and Evaluation: The AI tutor will be refined using this custom
dataset, ensuring that the final product is effective for the educational needs of each
child.
2
1.3 Modules
The project focuses on both technical development of AI models and the practical appli-
cation of these models.
1.3 Modules
1.3.1 Voice Interaction and Processing Module
This is the core module of the system that will handle the voice-based interaction and
processing of the little learner app.
1. Speech Recognition: Records and transcribes the children voice using our fine-
tuned whisper model, recognising the diverse patterns.
2. Answer Validation: Use LLM to properly analyze and understand the children’s
responses by using the context of the question.
3. Error Handling : Sometimes due to random reasons, the system is not able to
recognize the response it will ask to speak again.
1.3.2 Content Management and Personalization Module
This module will manage the content based on the kid’s preferences and interests. It will
also personalize the questions based on the difficulty level of the kid.
1. Dynamic Content Repository: Store and manage a library of educational content
that will be used to ask the questions.
2. Personalization: Customize the questions based on the difficulty level of the kid
that is being maintained with time.
3. Progress Tracking: Keep a record of the child’s progress and then change the
difficulty level of questions accordingly.
1.3.3 Data Collection and Processing
This module is responsible for collecting and making the dataset that will be used later to
fine-tune our transcription model. Collecting audio through a web page and then process-
ing and labeling those audio files will be done in this module.
1. Web-Based Audio Collection Interface: Ensure the interface is intuitive for users,
potentially children or their guardians, to submit audio samples.
3
1. Introduction
2. Audio Processing : Apply audio processing techniques to clean up the recordings,
such as noise reduction and volume normalization.
3. Data Labeling : Manually Label all the audio files.
1.3.4 Security and Scalability Infrastructure Module
Security is critical, especially when dealing with children’s data. This module ensures
that the platform is secure and scalable to handle varying loads and expand to new regions
without degradation in performance.
1. Data Encryption and Security : Implement robust encryption methods to secure
children’s data both at rest and in transit.
2. Scalable Architecture: Design the backend to scale resources dynamically based
on the demand, ensuring seamless performance during peak usage.
3. Analytics and Reporting: Provide tools for educators and parents to view detailed
reports on children’s progress and system usage.
1.3.5 Web Application Development
Creating and maintaining an interactive and attractive application for the kids to enjoy
themselves while using this application.
1. UI/UX: Beautiful design and flow to keep kids interested in learning through the
application.
2. Development: Complete design implementation according the UI/UX.
3. Integration: Integrate the model to work with the web application.
1.4 User Classes and Characteristics
Identify the various user classes that you anticipate will use this product, and describe
their pertinent characteristics.
4
1.4 User Classes and Characteristics
User class Description
Children
(End Users)
The "Little Learner" AI tutor is introduced with children of aged range of
3-7 years aiming at providing effective, interactive and fun learning con-
tent in accordance with individual’s level of understanding. This system
includes the method of recognizing and understanding children’s speech for
the preparation of educational content with the use of efficient voices that
would help enhance a child’s language, numbers, and mind in personalized
ways.
Parents /
Guardians
The operatives of the ’Little Learner’ system must include parents and
guardians of the child users. Their main concern would be to follow their
child’s educational progress and effectiveness of the content used. Parents
are familiar with the system and request security with data, settings regula-
tions, progress reviewing, assistance provision, and overall productive and
safe learning for children.
System Ad-
ministrators
The system administrators are the ones who ensure that the “Little Learner”
system functions correctly and is used safely. This function includes gover-
nance of the technical stack, maintenance of system availability, organiza-
tion of information security protection, and activity regulation considering
educational and information privacy aspects. They are in charge of per-
forming routine maintenance and providing technical support to the system
whenever it becomes necessary.

2.1 Use-case/Event Response Table/Storyboarding
2.1.1 Fully Dressed Use Cases
2.1.1.1 Use Case: Take Quiz
Primary Actor: Child
Secondary Actor: Parent/Guardian
Preconditions:
• The parent/guardian is registered and logged into the system.
• The parent/guardian has selected the child’s profile.
• The child has selected a course that includes quizzes.
Postconditions:
• The quiz results are recorded in the system.
• The child receives immediate feedback on their performance.
• The child’s progress is updated based on the quiz results.
Main Success Scenario:
1. The parent/guardian logs into the app.
2. The parent/guardian selects the child’s profile from multiple profiles (if applicable).
3. The child selects a specific course to engage with.
4. The child navigates to the quiz section of the selected course.
5. The child selects a quiz to take.
6. The system presents the quiz questions one by one.
7. The child submits answers to the quiz questions.
8. The system evaluates the answers in real-time.
9. The system provides immediate feedback on each question after submission.
10. Upon completing all questions, the system displays the quiz results and updates the
child’s progress.
9
2. Project Requirements
Alternative Flows:
A1. Multiple Attempts Allowed:
• If the quiz policy allows multiple attempts, the child can retake the quiz.
• Steps 6 through 10 of the Main Success Scenario are repeated.
Exception Cases:
E1. Incorrect Login Credentials:
• If the parent/guardian enters incorrect credentials, the system displays an error mes-
sage.
• The parent/guardian is prompted to try logging in again or to reset the password.
E2. Profile Selection Error:
• If there’s an error in selecting the child’s profile (e.g., system error, profile not
found), the system notifies the parent/guardian.
• The parent/guardian is prompted to try again or contact support.
E3. Course/Quiz Access Error:
• If the selected course/quiz is unavailable or locked, the system informs the child
and provides reasons (e.g., prerequisite not met, course/quiz under maintenance).
• The child (or parent/guardian) is advised to select another course or wait until ac-
cess is restored.
E4. No Response During Quiz:
• If the system detects no input from the child within a specific time, it prompts the
child to respond or offers to pause/exit the quiz.
• If no action is taken despite reminders, the quiz session is paused or ended based
on the system settings.
E5. Technical Issues (e.g., loss of connectivity):
• If there is a loss of connectivity during the quiz, the system attempts to save the last
recorded state.
• The child is prompted to reconnect. Upon reconnection, the child can resume from
the saved state or restart the quiz.
10
2.1 Use-case/Event Response Table/Storyboarding
2.1.1.2 Use Case: Answer Question
Primary Actor: Child
Preconditions:
• The child is logged into their profile.
• The child is actively engaged in a course or a quiz.
Postconditions:
• The child’s answer is recorded in the system.
• Feedback is provided based on the correctness of the answer.
• The child’s progress and performance data are updated.
Main Success Scenario:
1. The child is presented with a question, either as part of a quiz or interactive learning
session.
2. The system waits for the child’s response.
3. The child submits an answer using their voice.
4. The system uses speech recognition to transcribe the child’s answer.
5. The system evaluates the answer based on the question’s context and expected re-
sponse.
6. The system provides immediate feedback to the child:
• If the answer is correct, the system praises the child or encourages further
interaction.
• If the answer is incorrect, the system offers hints or explains the correct an-
swer.
7. The child’s response and the feedback are recorded in their learning progress.
Alternative Flows:
A1. Request for Repeat:
• If the child does not understand the question, they can request it to be repeated.
11
2. Project Requirements
• The system repeats the question without penalty.
Exception Cases:
E1. Speech Recognition Error:
• If the speech recognition system fails to accurately transcribe the child’s response,
the system prompts the child to repeat their answer.
• If repeated attempts fail, the system may suggest the child to type their answer if
possible.
E2. No Response Detected:
• If the system detects no response within a reasonable timeframe, it prompts the
child to answer.
• If there is still no response, the system may skip the question or end the session
based on the settings.
E3. Incorrect Response Format:
• If the child submits an answer in an incorrect format (e.g., typing in a voice-only
session), the system notifies the child of the proper format and requests a new an-
swer.
E4. System Error/Technical Issue:
• If a system error occurs during the question-answer session, the system attempts to
log the issue and may restart the session.
• The child is informed about the technical issue and is asked to retry or resume the
session later.
2.1.1.3 Use Case: Receive System Feedback
Primary Actor: Child
Preconditions:
• The child has submitted an answer to a question or completed a quiz.
• The system has evaluated the child’s answer or overall quiz performance.
12
2.1 Use-case/Event Response Table/Storyboarding
Postconditions:
• The child receives feedback that is informative and tailored to their response.
• The feedback contributes to the child’s learning progress and understanding.
Main Success Scenario:
1. After the child submits an answer, the system processes and evaluates the response.
2. The system determines the correctness of the answer:
• If correct, the system calculates and displays points earned and may provide
additional information or praise.
• If incorrect, the system explains why the answer was wrong and provides the
correct answer or a hint for better understanding.
3. The feedback is presented in a child-friendly manner, using positive reinforcement
to encourage learning.
4. If the feedback involves corrective information, it includes educational content to
help the child understand the correct concept.
5. The child listens to or views the feedback.
6. The feedback details (content, timing, and child’s response) are recorded for future
learning analytics and adaptive learning improvements.
Alternative Flows:
A1. Continuous Feedback During Quizzes:
• Instead of waiting until the end of a quiz, the system provides immediate feedback
after each question.
• This immediate feedback helps correct misconceptions early and maintains engage-
ment.
A2. Feedback Through Different Media:
• Depending on the child’s learning preference and the complexity of the topic, feed-
back is delivered via text, audio, or visual aids (like charts or animations).
Exception Cases:
E1. Feedback Misunderstanding:
13
2. Project Requirements
• If the child indicates or the system detects that the feedback was not understood
(e.g., the child repeats the mistake), the system offers to rephrase or provide addi-
tional examples.
• The system may invite the child to ask questions if they need more explanation.
E2. Technical Issues Affecting Feedback Delivery:
• If there is a delay or failure in delivering feedback due to technical issues, the system
informs the child of the issue and attempts to resolve it.
• If the issue persists, the system saves the session state and advises the child to restart
the application or come back later.
E3. Inaccurate Feedback:
• If erroneous feedback is given due to a system error in content or assessment logic,
the system logs the error for review.
• The child is advised that there was an error, and the correct feedback is provided
after a brief correction period.
2.1.1.4 Use Case: View Course / Take Course
Primary Actor: Child
Preconditions:
• The child is logged into their profile.
• The child has navigated to the course section of the app.
Postconditions:
• The child has accessed and interacted with the course content.
• The system has recorded the child’s interactions and progress for future reference
and adaptive learning adjustments.
Main Success Scenario:
1. The child selects a course from a list of available courses.
2. The system displays the course details, including a brief overview, learning objec-
tives, and any prerequisites.
14
2.1 Use-case/Event Response Table/Storyboarding
3. The child starts the course, which begins with an introductory video.
4. Throughout the video, there are interactive pauses where the video stops, and the
system asks the child questions related to the content just covered or about to be
covered.
5. The child responds to these interactive questions using voice inputs.
6. The system processes the child’s responses and provides immediate feedback:
• If the response is correct, the video continues with positive reinforcement.
• If the response is incorrect, the system provides the correct information and a
brief explanation before continuing.
7. The course includes several sections, each with its own set of videos and interactive
pauses.
8. The child’s progress, including their answers and the system’s feedback, is recorded
and analyzed for adaptive learning purposes.
Alternative Flows:
A1. Course Resumption:
• If the child exits the course midway, the system saves their progress.
• When the child returns, they are given the option to resume where they left off or
restart the course.
A2. Additional Learning Materials:
• After answering questions or at the end of a section, the child can access additional
learning materials such as worksheets or extended reading.
Exception Cases:
E1. Response Misinterpretation:
• If the system misinterprets the child’s voice response due to background noise or
unclear articulation, it prompts the child to repeat their answer.
• If issues persist, the system may offer the child the option to input their response
through an alternative method, such as selecting from multiple choices.
E2. Technical Issues with Video Playback:
15
2. Project Requirements
• If there are issues with video playback (e.g., buffering, errors), the system attempts
to resolve the issue and notifies the child of the delay.
• If the issue cannot be resolved promptly, the child is advised to try accessing the
course later, and the incident is logged for technical review.
E3. Lack of Engagement:
• If the system detects a lack of engagement (e.g., no responses to interactive ques-
tions, multiple incorrect answers suggesting distraction), it prompts the child with
motivational messages or hints to re-engage them.
• The system may also notify parents or educators if it observes consistent disengage-
ment, suggesting further intervention.
2.1.1.5 Use Case: Login/Register
Primary Actor: Parent/Guardian
Preconditions:
• The parent/guardian has access to the device with the app installed.
• The app is functioning properly without technical issues.
Postconditions:
• The parent/guardian is logged into the system, gaining access to manage their
child’s profile and view progress.
• If registering, the parent/guardian has created a new account with credentials stored
securely.
Main Success Scenario:
1. The parent/guardian opens the app and is presented with options to either log in or
register.
2. For Login:
(a) The parent/guardian enters their username and password.
(b) The system verifies the credentials and logs them into the system.
(c) The parent/guardian gains access to their dashboard where they can manage
profiles, view progress, and adjust settings.
16
2.1 Use-case/Event Response Table/Storyboarding
3. For Registration:
(a) The parent/guardian selects the register option.
(b) They are prompted to provide necessary information, including name, email
address, password, and contact details.
(c) They may also be asked to set up security questions or enable two-factor au-
thentication for enhanced security.
(d) After submitting the information, the system creates the account and sends a
confirmation email to the provided email address.
(e) The parent/guardian confirms their email address by clicking a link in the
email, which activates their account.
(f) Upon successful registration and email verification, the parent/guardian is
redirected to log in with their new credentials.
Alternative Flows:
A1. Forgotten Password:
• If a parent/guardian forgets their password, they select the “Forgot Password” op-
tion.
• They enter their email address, and the system sends them an email with a link to
reset their password.
• They follow the link, reset their password, and are then able to log in with the new
credentials.
A2. Update Profile Information:
• After logging in, the parent/guardian can access their account settings to update
profile information or change their password.
Exception Cases:
E1. Incorrect Credentials:
• If the entered credentials are incorrect, the system displays an error message.
• The parent/guardian is given the option to try logging in again or to reset their
password.
E2. Registration Errors:
17
2. Project Requirements
• If there is an issue during registration (e.g., missing information, weak password),
the system provides specific feedback about what needs to be corrected.
• The parent/guardian corrects the information and resubmits the form.
E3. Email Verification Issues:
• If the parent/guardian does not receive the confirmation email, they can request the
email be resent.
• If there is still an issue, they are advised to check their spam folder or contact
support for further assistance.
E4. Technical Problems:
• If there are technical issues preventing login or registration (e.g., server down, con-
nection error), the system notifies the parent/guardian of the issue and suggests
trying again later.
2.1.1.6 Use Case: Create/Manage Child Profile
Primary Actor: Parent/Guardian
Preconditions:
1. The parent/guardian is registered, logged in, and has verified their account.
2. The parent/guardian has access to the dashboard or profile management section of
the app.
Postconditions:
1. A new child profile is created, or existing profile information is updated success-
fully.
2. Changes are stored securely and are reflected immediately in the app for future
sessions.
Main Success Scenario:
1. The parent/guardian navigates to the “My Children” section of the app after logging
in.
2. For Creating a New Profile:
18
2.1 Use-case/Event Response Table/Storyboarding
(a) The parent/guardian clicks on “Add Child” or similar button.
(b) They are prompted to enter details about the child, such as name, age, educa-
tional level, interests, and any specific learning preferences or needs.
(c) They may also upload a photo or choose an avatar for the child’s profile.
(d) Upon entering all required information, the parent/guardian submits the form.
(e) The system processes and confirms the creation of the new child profile, pro-
viding a unique ID for the child’s profile within the app.
3. For Managing an Existing Profile:
(a) The parent/guardian selects an existing child’s profile from a list.
(b) They click on “Edit Profile” or a similar option.
(c) They update any details as needed, such as changing the child’s educational
level or updating interests.
(d) They can also deactivate or reactivate the profile, or update privacy settings.
(e) After making the desired changes, they submit the updates.
(f) The system processes and confirms the updates to the child’s profile.
Alternative Flows:
A1. Removing a Profile: If a parent/guardian decides to remove a child’s profile, they
select the delete option within the child’s profile settings. The system prompts
for confirmation to prevent accidental deletions. Upon confirmation, the profile is
deleted, and the system updates the list of profiles accordingly.
Exception Cases:
E1. Incomplete Profile Information: If essential information is missing when attempt-
ing to create or update a profile, the system displays an error message specifying
the missing data. The parent/guardian is prompted to complete these fields before
proceeding.
E2. Privacy Concerns: If the parent/guardian sets privacy settings that restrict certain
app functionalities, they are informed of these restrictions and their implications.
They must confirm these settings to proceed with their choices.
E3. Technical Issues: If there are technical issues (e.g., server errors, connectivity is-
sues) that prevent profile creation or updates, the system notifies the parent/guardian
and logs the issue. They are advised to try again later, and support may be contacted
if the problem persists.

2.3 Functional Requirements
2.3 Functional Requirements
2.3.1 Voice Interaction and Processing Module
1. Audio Input Capture: The system should be able to capture the kid’s voice clearly
for processing.
2. Noise Reduction: The system should be able to remove noise from the audio input
to give better feedback.
3. Feedback Generation: The system should be able to produce feedback according
to the processed voice. If the answer is correct, then it should encourage the kid. If
the answer is wrong, it should provide hints.
4. Response Re-prompting: The system should re-prompt the question again if the
kid fails to answer correctly or answer clearly after some attempts.
5. Logging Errors: The system should store logs of errors so that it can learn the
kid’s weak points and improve them.
2.3.2 Content Management and Personalization Module
1. Content Storage: The system should be able to store content for kids.
2. Content Updates: The system should be flexible to add new content or change
previous content.
3. Content Levels: The system should contain contents for each kid’s difficulty level.
It should contain content for easy, medium, and hard levels.
4. Progress Reports: The system should be able to display full report on the kid’s
performance like what is the kid’s weakness?
2.3.3 Data Collection and Processing
1. Recording Functionality : The interface should give users the functionality to
record audio files from any device with a microphone.
2. Noise Reduction: The collected audio files should be processed in order to remove
any unnecessary audio.
3. Volume Normalization: The audio files should have equal volume intensity for
better training.
23
2. Project Requirements
4. Quality Check : The audio files should be checked manually if the kid is even
saying the right words or not.
2.3.4 Security and Scalability Infrastructure Module
1. Progress Tracking Dashboard: The parents can view their child’s progress like
weaknesses and strengths.
2. Cloud Infrastructure: The system infrastructure will be run on cloud to ensure
scalability.
3. Secure Authentication: The system will ensure password protected entry.
4. Data Encryption: The data stored or transmitted should be encrypted in order to
improve security.
2.3.5 Web Application Development Module
1. Child-Friendly Design: The system should be very simple and intuitive to attract
the child.
2. Responsive Design: The system should be able to work on any device for more
accessibility.
2.4 Non-Functional Requirements
2.4.1 Reliability
Making the system accessible to everyone at everytime is very important. The system
should be running 24/7 to ensure access at any time. This will ensure that the app is
available to users at any time and will boost productivity.
2.4.2 Usability
USE-1: The application will be kept simple and ensures that users can access every sec-
tion within 2-3 clicks.
USE-2: The application will use visual cues and animations to guide users through sec-
tions and learning.
USE-3: The application will be able to provide feedback within 2-3 seconds after user
24
2.5 Domain Model
interaction.
2.4.3 Performance
PER-1: The application will ensure that about 90 percent of features respond in 3-5 sec-
onds.
PER-2: The system will be able to handle 5000 concurrent users at a time without any
delay.
PER-3: Audio processing requests will take almost 5-30 seconds to respond varying on
the audio quality.
PER-4: The system will ensure that 95 percent of learning content loads within 2-5 sec-
onds of user interaction, if the internet connection is fair.
2.4.4 Security
SEC-1: The system will be able to prevent unauthorized access to user data by encrypt-
ing it. To decrypt this information, it will be nearly impossible and brute-force attack will
take years.
SEC-2: The system will log all attempts of entry and set restrictions based on these at-
tempts. e.g if a person enters their password incorrect several times, he will be forced to
do 2 step-verification.
SEC-3: The system will be able to protect against common security threats like SQL
Injection etc. The success rate to stop these attacks will be about 90 percent.

# FYP1-MidReport-F24_121_D_GrociDish - i211361 Ahmed Mujtaba

## 1. Introduction

1. Introduction
1.2 Scope
The app will feature a personalized grocery list generation system that takes into account
user preferences, budget, and categories such as dietary needs. For managing recipes,
users will have access to a variety of pre-existing options with complete nutritional in-
formation, including those specifically tailored for conditions like diabetes, hypertension,
and obesity. Calories will be recommended based on each user’s Body Mass Index (BMI)
and Ideal Body Weight (IBW), ensuring portion control that aligns with individ- ual health
goals. Nutritional recommendations, including the balance of macro-nutrients (carbohy-
drates, fats, and proteins), will be adjusted according to the user’s activity levels. This will
optimize health outcomes by ensuring that the diet aligns with user’s lifestyle and specific
needs. The app will recommend recipes based on the ingredients available from the user’s
grocery list, providing a more customized experience. Additionally, the app will also fea-
ture daily caloric tracking for each family member, providing insights into their overall
consumption patterns for better health management. However, the app does not calculate
nutritional information in real time, meaning users will not receive immediate updates on
the nutritional content of their selected ingredients individually. Instead, the recipes will
have pre-calculated values. Similarly, the app does not generate new recipes on the fly
based on user inputs or available ingredients. Instead, it relies on a pre-existing database
of recipes, from which it suggests the most appropriate meal.
1.3 Modules
1.3.1 Personalized Grocery List Generation
This module enables users to generate grocery lists tailored to their preferences(like
brands) and budget,
1. Feature 1: Allows users to create grocery lists based on personal preferences .
2. Feature 2: Incorporates budget constraints to generate a cost-effective grocery list
that balances nutrition and affordability.
3. Feature 3: Provides brand preferences based on budget, allowing users to select
from local and affordable grocery brands for their purchases.
2
1.3 Modules
1.3.2 Grocery List Modification via Chatbot
This module integrates a chatbot that assists family members in managing their grocery
lists. The chatbot performs various operations such as retrieving, adding, and updating
items in the grocery list, ensuring an interactive and convenient experience for users.
1. Feature 1: Allows user to append items to their grocery list via a chat interface.
Users can simply input commands like "Add item".
2. Feature 2: Retrieves the current grocery list, providing users with an updated view
of the items already added.
3. Feature 3: Incorporates natural language processing to enhance user interaction,
making it easier to perform operations mentioned above.
1.3.3 Ingredient-Based Recipe Suggestions
This module recommends recipes based on the ingredients available from the generated
grocery list. It enhances customization by offering recipe suggestions that align with what
users already have, making meal planning more convenient and efficient.
1. Feature 1: Suggests recipes based on ingredients from the user’s grocery list, en-
suring minimal food waste and efficient meal planning.
2. Feature 2: Allows users to filter recipe suggestions by dietary preferences or health
conditions.
1.3.4 Meal Recommendation System
This module offers users a curated selection of recipes from the Calorics: Practical Man-
ual of Clinical Nutrition book, with detailed nutritional information. It also provides
recipes suited for health conditions (diabetes,hypertension and obesity).
1. Feature 1: Provides Meals tailored to health conditions like diabetes, hypertension,
and obesity, ensuring users can manage their dietary needs effectively.
2. Feature 2: Includes alternative Meal options for variety while maintaining nutri-
tional balance.
3. Feature 3: Nutritional calculations(Total Calories,Fats,carbs,protein and per serving
calories) are done with the help of a nutritionist to ensure accuracy and suitability
for local cuisine.
3
1. Introduction
4. Feature 4: Users can access a comprehensive database of pre-existing recipes.
1.3.5 Calories Recommendation System
This module calculates recommended serving sizes for each family member, ensuring
portion control tailored to individual health goals. It uses the Harris-Benedict equation to
personalize calorie and nutrient intake recommendations.
1. Feature 1: Takes user input including age, weight, height,gender and activity level.
2. Feature 2: Calculates BMI(Body Mass Index) and IBW(Ideal Body Weight) for
accurate health assessments.
3. Feature 3: Uses the Harris-Benedict equation to calculate daily calorie needs based
on the individual’s activity level, aiding in meal planning.
4. Feature 4: Recommends for each family member, detailing daily calories, carbohy-
drates, protein, and fat intake for optimal nutrition.
1.3.6 Caloric Tracking
This module tracks the daily caloric intake of each family member, providing insights
into overall consumption and helping users monitor their diet to make healthier choices.
It allows for personalized tracking with optional privacy controls.
1. Feature 1: Each family member can have an optional, password-protected calorie
tracker, ensuring privacy and individualized monitoring.
2. Feature 2: Tracks daily caloric intake giving users a comprehensive view of their
diet.
3. Feature 4: Provides a visual breakdown of calorie sources, making it easier for users
to identify unhealthy eating patterns or nutrient deficiencies.

2.5 Non-Functional Requirements
This section specifies nonfunctional requirements of the GrociDish App.
2.5.1 Reliability
Error Handling: The system should manage expected and unexpected errors efficiently,
providing users with clear error messages, such as when a grocery list or recipe fails to
generate.
Fault Tolerance: GrociDish should handle failures like a service or database crash by
using retries and backups for key processes, including grocery list generation and recipe
recommendations.
2.5.2 Usability
Ease of Use: The app interface (React Native) must be intuitive and user-friendly, allow-
ing easy navigation for functions like updating grocery lists and viewing recipes. .
Interoperability: Usability requirements ensure ease of use, error prevention, efficient
interaction, and accessibility for all users. These requirements help design an optimal
user experience.
2.5.3 Performance
Data Processing: GrociDish must handle real-time calorie tracking and ingredient up-
dates as meals are consumed by family members.
27
2. Project Requirements
Background Tasks: Weekly scraping of grocery prices must not affect real-time perfor-
mance and should be processed asynchronously.
• PER-1: The system shall generate a personalized grocery list within 10 seconds
after user input.
• PER-2: Updates to the grocery list shall be reflected in real-time, with changes
processed in less than 10 seconds.
• PER-3: The chatbot shall respond to user queries within 5 seconds.
• PER-4: The system shall suggest Meal plan based on ingredients within 10 sec-
onds.
• PER-5: Calorie recommendations shall be processed within 5 seconds.
• PER-6: BMI and IBW calculations shall achieve 95% accuracy based on user
data.
• PER-7: Calorie breakdowns shall be displayed within5 seconds after user request.
• PER-8: Login requests shall be processed within 3 seconds.
2.5.4 Security
Authorization and Role Management: Only authorized users should generate grocery
lists or select Meal Plans. Each family member should have appropriate role-based access.
Authentication: The system should implement secure login with JWT, ensuring token
expiration and revocation mechanisms. Security requirements should protect the system
and its data from unauthorized access, defining the effort required to breach the system.

Chapter 3
System Overview
3.1 Architectural Design
The architecture diagram for GrociDish shows the interaction between the frontend ap-
plication, backend services, and the AI models. Users access the system through the
frontend application, which communicates with the API Gateway. The gateway acts
as the central hub for routing requests to the various backend services, including User
& Family Management, Grocery List Service, Meal Tracking Service, Recipe Man-
agement, and the Chatbot Service. These services interact with a database to manage
users, recipes, and grocery data. TheAI services — specifically the Grocery Generation
Model and the Recipe Suggestion Model — provide tailored recommendations for gro-
ceries and recipes, enhancing the app’s functionality based on user data. The architecture
ensures a modular design with AI integration for personalized recommendations.
3.1.1 Architectural Diagram
31
3. System Overview
Figure 3.1: GrociDish Architectural Diagram
3.2 Design Models
Approach:Object Oriented Development Approach
OOP is better suited for GrociDish because it organizes the system into reusable, modular
components that represent real-world entities like users, recipes, and grocery lists. This
structure enhances maintainability and scalability as the app grows, allowing features like
calorie tracking, recipe selection, and family member management to be handled effi-
ciently within separate classes. OOP also enables encapsulation, securing sensitive data
like user health information, and supports re-usability through inheritance, making it eas-
ier to extend functionality in the future without major rewrites. This flexibility makes
OOP ideal for a complex, evolving system like GrociDish.

1.2 Scope
The scope of the V ocalCraftproject includes:
• V oice Command Functionality: This comprises a voice recognition model which
is to transcribe the user’s voice instructions in controlling the aspects of the pam-
phlet design such as layout, images, and text content.
• Image Generation using Diffusion Model: It integrates the Diffusion Model,
which automates the generation of visual and textual content to make pamphlets
attractive-looking with correctly and professionally styled text according to user
prompts.
• Customizable Templates: To this end, the application will grant users the ability
to edit and finalize pamphlets that have been generated according to their brand
identity and marketing requirements.
• Print-Ready Output: With the application, the user can create pamphlets ready
for printing. These could be exported into numerous formats such that sharing is
easy, even in SVG format.
• Cloud-Based Storage: This lets the user save their projects online. Cloud solutions
enable them to access and manipulate their projects from anywhere; thus, much
convenience is achieved with such an option.
• Mobile App Accessibility: It will make the system accessible through the use of
mobile applications. The system will therefore have a wider usability scale for
different users.
• User-Friendly Interface: Creating a user-first interface that will ease the process
of creating pamphlets, making it friendly for users with minimum design experience
to produce quality promotional materials.

The following comprises of modules of V ocalCraft
1.3.1 V oice Input and Transcription Module
Purpose: Convert the user’s voice input into text for further processing.

1.3 Modules
• Capture voice input from the user through a mobile app.

1.3.2 Prompt Engineering and NER Module
Purpose: Perform prompt engineering and extract key entities for pamphlet design.
Key Functionality:
• Extract important details (e.g., colors, text, layout) using Named Entity Recognition
(NER).
• Store extracted entities to guide image generation and layout.
1.3.3 Image Generation and Inpainting Module
Purpose: Generate the pamphlet image with placeholders for text and images using in-
painting.
Key Functionality:
• Use the diffusion model to generate the main pamphlet image based on NER data.
• Incorporate inpainting to add placeholders for text and object areas.
• Inference-time inpainting using a binary mask to place text and objects precisely.
1.3.4 Segmentation and Binary Mask Generation Module
Purpose: Create a binary mask to define areas for text and images on the pamphlet.
Key Functionality:
• Generate a binary mask for the text and image areas.
• Remove the objects from the final output image using
• Feed this mask into the diffusion model for inpainting.

2.2 Functional Requirements
This section describes the functional requirements of V ocalCraft.
2.2.1 User Authentication
The following are the requirements for User Authentication:
1. The users will sign up using their email, password, and username.
2. The system will authenticate the user.
3. The system shall allow users to log out of their accounts.
23
2. Project Requirements
2.2.2 V oice-to-Text Processing
The following are the requirements for V oice-to-Text Processing:
1. The system shall use the user’s microphone for voice input.
2. The system shall convert the voice input into text using a speech recognition model
(Whisper).
3. The system shall display the text to the user for confirmation or editing.
2.2.3 Text Input
The following are the requirements for Text Input:
1. The system shall allow users to input text prompts for poster generation manually.
2. The system shall validate the text input to ensure it is not empty and make sure no
information is missing (e.g., background color).
2.2.4 Poster Generation
The following are the requirements for Poster Generation:
1. The system shall generate a poster based on the user’s voice or text prompts using
an image generation model (Stable Diffusion).
2. The system shall present the generated poster to the user for preview.
2.2.5 Poster Editing
The following are the requirements for Poster Editing:
1. The system shall allow users to edit the generated poster (e.g., move text, change
colours, and fonts, and resize elements).
2. The system shall apply user edites to the poster in real-time.
3. The system shall allow users to undo changes made during the editing process.
24
2.3 Non-Functional Requirements
2.2.6 Save and Load Prompts
The following are the requirements for Save and Load Prompts:
1. The system shall enable the users to save their voice or text prompts for later use.
2. The system shall allow users to retrieve previuosly saved prompts and reuse them
to generate posters.
2.2.7 Save and Share Posters
The following are the requirements for Save and Share Posters:
1. The system shall permit the users to download generated or edited posters in con-
ventional formats such as PNG or JPEG.
2. The system shall allow users to share the generated posters directly via social media,
email, or messaging apps.
2.2.8 System Monitoring and Management (Admin Features)
The following are the requirements for System Monitoring and Management:
1. The system shall enable an admin to monitor system performance of such functions
as usage of the poster generation and user activity.
2. The system shall enable an admin to create user profiles, delete and modify users’
access permission.
3. This system shall be capable to log errors and enable the admin to view log in case
of troublshooting.
2.3 Non-Functional Requirements
This section specifies the nonfunctional requirements of V ocalCraft.
2.3.1 Reliability
1. It is required that the Mean Time Between Failure (MTBF) should not be below
1000 hours of uninterrupted usage.
25
2. Project Requirements
2. They defined a system failure as the inability to produce or generate a poster, re-
spond to user input, or operate with voice/text prompts.
3. If failure happens, the system should be able to recognize that or at least recognize
the right time to alert the admin and the user.
4. The system must have automatic recovery mechanisms such as retries for poster
generation or a fallback strategy in case of a system failure.
5. The system has to produce and store failure logs.
6. The admin needs to be informed of consecutive failures that occur and records need
to be kept of all system failures to analyze further.
2.3.2 Usability
1. USE-1: The system shall allow users to generate a poster from a voice or text
prompt with no more than 3 interactions.
2. USE-2: The system’s interface must allow users to preview the generated poster
and make edits (e.g., adjust text or colors) with drag-and-drop functionality.
3. USE-3: The system must provide real-time visual feedback during poster genera-
tion, including progress bars or loading indicators.
4. USE-4: The system shall allow users to undo or reset any poster edits with a single
interaction.
5. USE-6: The system must provide tips for first-time users to help them understand
the main features.
2.3.3 Performance
1. PER-1: 95% of posters generated by the system shall be completed within 30 sec-
onds from the time the user submits the request, under normal operating conditions.
2. PER-2: 90% of voice-to-text conversions shall be processed and displayed to the
user within 5 seconds after submission of voice input.
3. PER-3: The system must support at least 100 concurrent users generating posters
without noticeable performance degradation (response times not exceeding 30 sec-
onds).
26
2.3 Non-Functional Requirements
4. PER-4: The system shall allow the user to save or share posters within 5 seconds
after the user confirms the poster is finalized.
5. PER-5: In case of heavy load (more than 100 concurrent users), the system must
maintain a response time of less than 60 seconds for 80% of poster generation
requests.
2.3.4 Security
1. SEC-1: The system shall prevent unauthorized access to user data and poster gener-
ation history, requiring a high level of technical skill and significant time to breach.
2. SEC-2: The system shall detect and log any unauthorized access attempts and no-
tify the admin within 5 minutes of the detection.
3. SEC-3: The system shall ensure that user data is protected against unauthorized
modifications, and any changes to sensitive data must be traceable.
4. SEC-4: The system shall maintain the integrity of poster data during transmission
and storage to prevent data corruption.
27

System Overview
3.1 Architectural Design
The architectural design followed by V ocalCraft will be API-Centric Microservices with
Event-Driven Workflow. This architecture offers flexibility, maintainability, and scalabil-
ity. Most of the core functionalities of the app are abstracted within the external APIs
and the app provides a procedural event-based workflow to the user. Event-based en-
sures smooth asynchronous communication between services, allowing the user real-time
interaction with the app. This architecture is also secure due to the API gateways, and
token-based authentication that protects the sensitive operations of the app and user’s
data.

3. System Overview
3.3 Data Design
3.3.1 Information Domain Transformation
It verifies the user’s credentials for authentication. Then the user speaks to the system or
writes a text input, and the system translates it into the pamphlet of the user requirement.
The input is then passed through external API such as STT conversion, prompt engineer-
ing, JSON prompts file, image generation, and image inpainting. The data generated is
used to construct the image, and the user can also share and or download the image.
1. System Inputs
• User Login Credentials (Text Input)
• Image Prompt (V oice/Text Input)
2. System Output
• Generated Image
• Sharing and Downloading options
The app converts the voice prompt to text using an external speaker-to-text translation
application. Once in text form, the given textual prompt is refined, and transformed into
a JSON file using the Prompt Engineering and the NER API which defines key entities.
Later, the prompt is transferred to the Diffusion API to generate the image and its parts in
order to obtain the result.
3.3.2 Data Structures
The data entities in the app are for the user entries, user prompts, and generated images.
1. User Entity
• Attributes: ID, Name, Email, Password
• Description: Stores the user login and signup information
2. User Prompts
• Attributes: ID, Prompt
• Description: Stores the user prompts.
3. Image Entity
44
3.3 Data Design
• Attributes: ID, Name, ImageData, DateCreated, ShareData
• Description: Stores metadata for each image generated by the user, including
whether it was shared or downloaded.
Relationships:
• There is a one to many mapping between entities User to Image because one
instance of a user can provide many images.
• A one-to-many relationship exists between User and User Prompts since 1
user can generate many prompts.
• A one-to-one relationship exists between User Prompts and Image entities,
as one prompt by a single user will generate only one image.
3.3.3 Storage Mechanisms
The app uses a relational database to store user credentials and image data securely.
• Database:
– Type: Relational Database (MySQL)
– Purpose: To store user login details, user prompts, and generated images.
• Storage Strategy:
– User data is stored in a secure table with hashed passwords.
– Prompt Data is stored in the table linked with the User table, organized by
time created, and could be filtered out for each user.
– Image data is stored along with metadata, including creation date and user
ID for easy retrieval.
3.3.4 Data Processing
After the user login, the app processes the input prompt through several stages:
• V oice-to-Text Conversion:The voice input is then passed to an API outside this
system where it is converted into text.
• Prompt Engineering and NER Processing: The subsequent text is processed us-
ing a Prompt Engineering and a Named Entity Recognition API to perform Key
Entity Extraction.
45
3. System Overview
• Image Generation: The extracted entities are then employed to create an image
via another API.
Concurrency and Transactions:
• Transactions for user login and image generation are handled in a sequential pro-
cess, ensuring each stage is completed before moving to the next.
• Session management is used to maintain user states, ensuring they remain logged
in during the process.
3.3.5 Security and Integrity of Data
Data security is maintained throughout the app, particularly for sensitive user information
and images.
• Data Protection: User passwords are securely hashed, and sensitive data is en-
crypted in storage.
• Access Control: Before accessing the app’s features, user authentication will be
required. Users and an admin will have different accesses in the app.
3.3.6 Data Flow Between Subsystems
The app consists of several subsystems:
• User Authentication Subsystem: Handles user login and sign up.
• STT Subsystem: Transforms voice prompts into text using an STT API.
• Prompt Engineering and NER Subsystem: Extracts entities set in a pre-defined
prompt layout from the text prompt.
• Image Generation Subsystem: Creates images and components within the images
based on extracted entities using an external API.
• Image Sharing/Download Subsystem: Allows users to share or download the
generated image.
These subsystems follow a sequential data flow from getting the user input prompt to
generating the pamphlet.
46
3.3 Data Design
3.3.7 Optimization and Performance Considerations
The app’s performance will be a result of the following optimizations:
• Asynchronous API Calls: API calls for voice-to-text conversion and NER pro-
cessing are handled asynchronously to reduce latency.
47
Chapter 4
Conclusion
In conclusion, through the application of voice-to-text processing, prompt engineering,
and diffusion models, the V ocalCraftwill showcase its capability to completely trans-
form the way promotional pamphlet is done. Being an app for mobile use and having
customizable templates and cloud storage, V ocalCraft offers fast-food businesses an ef-
fective and easily affordable tool for their marketing work.
Such voice input and text output modules, along with the image processing module guar-
antee the attractiveness and, at the same time, professional appearance of the text pre-
sented in the proposed pamphlets. Concerning the current market, the project directly
responds to some of the principal problems encountered with graphic design tools be-
cause it provides straightforward software that does not require any prior experience in
design.
Also as V ocalCraft gradually transitions to a real-world application, there will be a need
to improve the performance, accessibility, and security of the system as it forms a com-
petitive tool for the marketing industry.
48
Glossary
ASR (Automatic Speech Recognition): A technology that converts spoken language into
text using algorithms and machine learning models. In this report, it refers to the
use of the Whisper model to transcribe voice input.
Diffusion Model: A generative model that generates images by iterative denoising a ran-
dom noise image based on a given input prompt. Used for creating images in V o-
calCraft’s poster generation process.
NER (Named Entity Recognition): A process in natural language processing (NLP) that
identifies and classifies key information (entities) such as names, dates, and loca-
tions from text data. In this project, it helps extract relevant details from user input
to generate the pamphlet content.
Prompt Engineering: The process of designing input prompts for AI systems to guide
their output. In this project, the Prompt Engineering will done on the textual prompt
for the diffusion models to create a pamphlet.
Inpainting: A technique used to fill in missing or masked parts of an image. In the
V ocalCraft project, inpainting is used to add placeholders for text and object areas
within the generated image.
STT (Speech-to-Text): A technology that converts spoken language into written text.
Whisper, an STT model, will be used in this project to transcribe the user’s voice
input into text.

## 1. Introduction

1. Introduction
ensuring that the transition from 2D images to 3D models is accurate and lifelike. Users
will have access to a wide range of customization options to modify their avatars, such as
adjusting skin tone, hairstyles, facial structure, and facial hair. These customization tools
ensure that users can create avatars that closely reflect their personal appearance or their
desired digital representation.
A core feature of Personix is the ability to animate avatars with natural facial expressions
and movements. The platform will also integrate text-to-speech functionality, allowing
avatars to speak in a realistic manner. Sentiment analysis will further enhance the experi-
ence by enabling avatars to display appropriate emotions based on the text input provided
by the user. Users will be able to generate content such as stickers, GIFs, and short videos
(30 seconds to 1 minute), which can easily be shared across various social media plat-
forms.
In addition to creating a single avatar, the platform will support the creation and man-
agement of multiple avatars, allowing users to switch between different representations
as needed. Users will also have the option to apply their avatars as face filters for video
recordings, making the platform even more interactive. Cloud storage functionality will
ensure that avatars are securely stored and synchronized across devices, providing users
with the flexibility to access their avatars from anywhere. In summary, the scope of Per-
sonix includes 3D modeling, avatar customization, animation, text-to-speech integration,
content creation tools, and cloud synchronization, all designed to enhance digital self-
representation while maintaining user privacy and flexibility.
